title: Philadelphia

infobox.value: [Kingdom of England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") Netherlands Sweden [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape")

[13th](/wiki/List_of_North_American_cities_by_population "List of North American cities by population") in North America [6th](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States [1st](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Pennsylvania "List of municipalities in Pennsylvania") in Pennsylvania

([/ˌfɪləˈdɛlfiə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Philadelphia.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Philadelphia.wav") [*FIL-ə-DEL-fee-ə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), colloquially referred to as **Philly**, is the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Pennsylvania "List of municipalities in Pennsylvania") in the U.S. state of [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"),[[10]](#cite_note-United_States_Census_Bureau-2-10) and the [sixth-most populous city in the United States](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population"), with a [Census](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau")-estimated population of 1,574,281 in July 2025.[[11]](#cite_note-PhiladelphiaQuickFacts-11)

**Philadelphia** ([/ˌfɪləˈdɛlfiə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q1860_(eng)-Flame,_not_lame-Philadelphia.wav "File:LL-Q1860 (eng)-Flame, not lame-Philadelphia.wav") [*FIL-ə-DEL-fee-ə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")), colloquially referred to as **Philly**, is the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Pennsylvania "List of municipalities in Pennsylvania") in the U.S. state of [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"),[[10]](#cite_note-United_States_Census_Bureau-2-10) and the [sixth-most populous city in the United States](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population"), with a [Census](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau")-estimated population of 1,574,281 in July 2025.[[11]](#cite_note-PhiladelphiaQuickFacts-11)
The Philadelphia metropolitan area (also called the [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley")) has 6.33 million residents and is the nation's [ninth-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area").[[12]](#cite_note-12) Philadelphia is known for [its culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Philadelphia "Culture of Philadelphia"), [cuisine](/wiki/Philadelphia_cuisine "Philadelphia cuisine"), and [history](/wiki/History_of_Philadelphia "History of Philadelphia"), maintaining contemporary influence in [business and technology](/wiki/Economy_of_Philadelphia "Economy of Philadelphia"), sports, and music.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Philadelphia was founded in 1682 by [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"), an English [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers") and advocate of [religious freedom](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion"), and served as the capital of the colonial era [Province of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Province_of_Pennsylvania "Province of Pennsylvania").[[3]](#cite_note-W._W._Norton_&_Company-1982-3)[[15]](#cite_note-15) It then played a vital role during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") and [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). It served as the central meeting place for the [nation's Founding Fathers](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States") in hosting the [First Continental Congress](/wiki/First_Continental_Congress "First Continental Congress") (1774) and the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress"), during which the Founders formed the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army"), elected [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") as its commander, and adopted the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") on July 4, 1776. During the Revolutionary War's [Philadelphia campaign](/wiki/Philadelphia_campaign "Philadelphia campaign"), the city fell to the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_American_Revolutionary_War "British Army during the American Revolutionary War"), which occupied Philadelphia for nine months from September 1777 to June 1778.[[16]](#cite_note-Ellis-2007-16) Following the end of the Revolutionary War, the [U.S. Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States") was ratified at the [Philadelphia Convention](/wiki/Constitutional_Convention_(United_States) "Constitutional Convention (United States)"). Philadelphia remained the nation's largest city until 1790, and it served as the nation's first capital from May 10, 1775, until December 12, 1776, and on [four subsequent occasions](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") until 1800, when construction of the new national capital in Washington, D.C. was completed.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

With [17 four-year universities and colleges](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_Philadelphia "List of colleges and universities in Philadelphia") in the city, Philadelphia is one of the nation's leading centers for higher education and academic research.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-Sisak-2015-19) The city hosts more outdoor sculptures and murals than any other city in the nation.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Fairmount Park](/wiki/Fairmount_Park "Fairmount Park"), when combined with adjacent [Wissahickon Valley Park](/wiki/Wissahickon_Valley_Park "Wissahickon Valley Park") in the same [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin"), has an area of 2,052 acres (830 ha), representing one of the nation's largest urban parks and the [world's 55th largest](/wiki/List_of_urban_parks_by_size "List of urban parks by size").[[22]](#cite_note-tpl.org-22) With [five professional sports teams](/wiki/Sports_in_Philadelphia "Sports in Philadelphia") and one of the nation's most loyal and passionate fan bases, Philadelphia is often ranked as the nation's best city for professional sports fans.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) The city has a culturally and [philanthropically](/wiki/Philanthropy "Philanthropy") active [LGBTQ+ community](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_Philadelphia "LGBT culture in Philadelphia"). Philadelphia also has played an [influential historic and ongoing role](/wiki/Music_of_Philadelphia "Music of Philadelphia") in the development and evolution of American music, especially [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), and rock.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philadelphia&action=edit), the Philadelphia metropolitan area had a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/List_of_U.S._metropolitan_areas_by_GDP "List of U.S. metropolitan areas by GDP") of US$557.6 billion[[29]](#cite_note-fred.stlouisfed.org-29) and is home to 13 [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") corporate headquarters.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Metropolitan Philadelphia ranks as one of the nation's Big Five [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") hubs, facilitated by its proximity to both the [financial ecosystems](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") of New York City and the regulatory environment of Washington, D.C.[[31]](#cite_note-Maloney-2024-31) Metropolitan Philadelphia is also a [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") hub and has garnered the nickname "Cellicon Valley" for its central role in the development of [immunotherapies](/wiki/Immunotherapy "Immunotherapy") to treat different cancers.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The [Philadelphia Stock Exchange](/wiki/Philadelphia_Stock_Exchange "Philadelphia Stock Exchange"), owned by [Nasdaq](/wiki/Nasdaq "Nasdaq") since 2008, is the nation's oldest stock exchange and a global leader in [options](/wiki/Option_(finance) "Option (finance)") trading.[[33]](#cite_note-Lucas_Downey_and_Somer_Anderson-2022-33) [30th Street Station](/wiki/30th_Street_Station "30th Street Station"), the city's primary rail station, is the [third-busiest Amtrak hub](/wiki/List_of_busiest_Amtrak_stations "List of busiest Amtrak stations") in the nation with over 4.1 million passengers in 2023. The city's [multimodal transportation](/wiki/Multimodal_transportation "Multimodal transportation") and logistics infrastructure includes [Philadelphia International Airport](/wiki/Philadelphia_International_Airport "Philadelphia International Airport"),[[34]](#cite_note-34) the [PhilaPort seaport](/wiki/Port_of_Philadelphia "Port of Philadelphia"),[[35]](#cite_note-35) and [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95_(Pennsylvania) "Interstate 95 (Pennsylvania)"), a primary component of the north–south highway system along the [U.S. East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States").

Philadelphia is a city of many firsts, including the nation's first [library](/wiki/Library_Company_of_Philadelphia "Library Company of Philadelphia") (1731),[[36]](#cite_note-USHistory-36) [hospital](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Hospital "Pennsylvania Hospital") (1751),[[36]](#cite_note-USHistory-36) [medical school](/wiki/Perelman_School_of_Medicine_at_the_University_of_Pennsylvania "Perelman School of Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania") (1765),[[37]](#cite_note-37) [national capital](/wiki/First_Continental_Congress "First Continental Congress") (1774),[[38]](#cite_note-38) [university (by some accounts)](/wiki/First_university_in_the_United_States "First university in the United States") (1779),[[39]](#cite_note-39) [central bank](/wiki/Bank_of_North_America "Bank of North America") (1781),[[40]](#cite_note-40) [stock exchange](/wiki/Philadelphia_Stock_Exchange "Philadelphia Stock Exchange") (1790),[[36]](#cite_note-USHistory-36) [zoo](/wiki/Philadelphia_Zoo "Philadelphia Zoo") (1874),[[41]](#cite_note-41) and [business school](/wiki/Wharton_School_of_the_University_of_Pennsylvania "Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania") (1881).[[42]](#cite_note-42) Philadelphia contains 67 [National Historic Landmarks](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Philadelphia "List of National Historic Landmarks in Philadelphia"), including [Independence Hall](/wiki/Independence_Hall "Independence Hall").[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[19]](#cite_note-Sisak-2015-19) From the city's 17th century founding through the present, Philadelphia has been the birthplace of or home to many [prominent and influential Americans](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Philadelphia "List of people from Philadelphia").

## History

Main article: [History of Philadelphia](/wiki/History_of_Philadelphia "History of Philadelphia")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Philadelphia](/wiki/Timeline_of_Philadelphia "Timeline of Philadelphia").

See also: [List of National Historic Landmarks in Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Philadelphia "List of National Historic Landmarks in Philadelphia") and [National Register of Historic Places listings in Philadelphia](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Philadelphia "National Register of Historic Places listings in Philadelphia")

### Native peoples

Prior to the arrival of Europeans in the early 17th century, the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), an [Indian tribe](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") also known as the Delaware Indians, lived in the village of [Shackamaxon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Shackamaxon "Treaty of Shackamaxon") in present-day Philadelphia and the surrounding area.[[45]](#cite_note-Josephy_188-45) The Lenape historically lived along the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin"), western [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), and the [Lower Hudson Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley").[[a]](#cite_note-46) Most Lenape were pushed out of the region during the 18th century as the original [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") expanded, which was further exacerbated by losses from intertribal conflicts.[[45]](#cite_note-Josephy_188-45) Lenape communities were also weakened by newly introduced diseases, mainly [smallpox](/wiki/Smallpox "Smallpox"), and conflicts with Europeans. The [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") occasionally fought the Lenape. Surviving Lenape moved west into the upper [Ohio River](/wiki/Ohio_River "Ohio River") basin. Following the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") and the subsequent establishment of the United States, the Lenape began moving further west. In the 1860s, the [U.S. federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") sent most remaining Lenape in the [eastern United States](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") to the [Indian Territory](/wiki/Indian_Territory "Indian Territory") in present-day [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") and surrounding territories as part of the [Indian removal](/wiki/Indian_removal "Indian removal") policy.

### Colonial era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/The_Birth_of_Pennsylvania_1680_cph.3g07157.jpg/250px-The_Birth_of_Pennsylvania_1680_cph.3g07157.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Birth_of_Pennsylvania_1680_cph.3g07157.jpg)

[William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn") (holding paper) and [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") depicted in *The Birth of Pennsylvania 1680*, a portrait by [Jean Leon Gerome Ferris](/wiki/Jean_Leon_Gerome_Ferris "Jean Leon Gerome Ferris")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Treaty_of_Penn_with_Indians_by_Benjamin_West.jpg/250px-Treaty_of_Penn_with_Indians_by_Benjamin_West.jpg)](/wiki/File:Treaty_of_Penn_with_Indians_by_Benjamin_West.jpg)

William Penn's 1682 [Treaty of Shackamaxon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Shackamaxon "Treaty of Shackamaxon") with the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") tribe depicted in *[Penn's Treaty with the Indians](/wiki/Penn%27s_Treaty_with_the_Indians "Penn's Treaty with the Indians")*, a 1772 portrait by [Benjamin West](/wiki/Benjamin_West "Benjamin West")

[![A Portraiture of the City of Philadelphia, by Thomas Holme](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Map_of_the_Original_City_of_Philadelphia_in_1682_by_Thomas_Holme.jpg/250px-Map_of_the_Original_City_of_Philadelphia_in_1682_by_Thomas_Holme.jpg)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Original_City_of_Philadelphia_in_1682_by_Thomas_Holme.jpg)

A 1683 portrait of Philadelphia by [Thomas Holme](/wiki/Thomas_Holme "Thomas Holme"), believed to be the city's first map

Europeans first entered Philadelphia and the surrounding [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") in the early 17th century. The first settlements were founded by [Dutch colonists](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas"), who built [Fort Nassau](/wiki/Fort_Nassau_(South_River) "Fort Nassau (South River)") on the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") in 1623 in what is now [Brooklawn, New Jersey](/wiki/Brooklawn,_New_Jersey "Brooklawn, New Jersey"). The Dutch considered the entire Delaware River valley to be part of their [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") colony. In 1638, Swedish settlers led by renegade Dutch established the colony of [New Sweden](/wiki/New_Sweden "New Sweden") at [Fort Christina](/wiki/Fort_Christina "Fort Christina"), located in present-day [Wilmington, Delaware](/wiki/Wilmington,_Delaware "Wilmington, Delaware"), and quickly spread out in the valley. In 1644, New Sweden supported the [Susquehannocks](/wiki/Susquehannock "Susquehannock") in their war against [Maryland](/wiki/Province_of_Maryland "Province of Maryland") colonists.[[46]](#cite_note-47) In 1648, the Dutch built [Fort Beversreede](/wiki/Fort_Beversreede "Fort Beversreede") on the west bank of the Delaware, south of the [Schuylkill River](/wiki/Schuylkill_River "Schuylkill River") near the present-day [Eastwick](/wiki/Eastwick,_Philadelphia "Eastwick, Philadelphia") section of Philadelphia, to reassert their dominion over the area. The [Swedes](/wiki/Swedes "Swedes") responded by building [Fort Nya Korsholm](/wiki/New_Sweden#Forts "New Sweden"), or New [Korsholm](/wiki/Korsholm "Korsholm"), named after a town in Finland with a Swedish majority.

In 1655, a [Dutch military](/wiki/Dutch_armed_forces "Dutch armed forces") campaign led by New Netherland Director-General [Peter Stuyvesant](/wiki/Peter_Stuyvesant "Peter Stuyvesant") took control of the Swedish colony, ending its claim to independence. The Swedish and [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_people "Finnish people") settlers continued to have their own [militia](/wiki/Militia "Militia"), religion, and court, and to enjoy substantial autonomy under the Dutch. An English fleet captured the New Netherland colony in 1664, though the situation did not change substantially until 1682, when the area was included in [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn")'s charter for Pennsylvania.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

In 1681, in partial repayment of a debt, [Charles II of England](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") granted Penn a [charter](/wiki/Charter "Charter") for what would become the [Pennsylvania colony](/wiki/Province_of_Pennsylvania "Province of Pennsylvania"). Despite the royal charter, Penn bought the land from the local [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape") in an effort to establish good terms with the Native Americans and ensure peace for the colony.[[48]](#cite_note-49) Penn made a [treaty of friendship](/wiki/Treaty_of_Shackamaxon "Treaty of Shackamaxon") with Lenape chief [Tammany](/wiki/Tamanend "Tamanend") under an elm tree at [Shackamaxon](/wiki/Shackamaxon "Shackamaxon"), in what is now the city's [Fishtown](/wiki/Fishtown,_Philadelphia "Fishtown, Philadelphia") neighborhood.[[3]](#cite_note-W._W._Norton_&_Company-1982-3) Penn named the city Philadelphia, which is Greek for 'brotherly love', derived from the [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") terms [φίλος](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CF%86%CE%AF%CE%BB%CE%BF%CF%82 "wikt:φίλος") *phílos* ('beloved, dear') and [ἀδελφός](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%80%CE%B4%CE%B5%CE%BB%CF%86%CF%8C%CF%82 "wikt:ἀδελφός") *adelphós* ('brother, brotherly'). There were a number of cities named *[Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia_(disambiguation)#Ancient "Philadelphia (disambiguation)")* ([Φιλαδέλφεια](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%A6%CE%B9%CE%BB%CE%B1%CE%B4%CE%AD%CE%BB%CF%86%CE%B5%CE%B9%CE%B1 "wikt:Φιλαδέλφεια")) in the [Eastern Mediterranean](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean "Eastern Mediterranean") during the Greek and Roman periods, including modern [Alaşehir](/wiki/Ala%C5%9Fehir "Alaşehir"), mentioned as the site of an early Christian congregation in the [Book of Revelation](/wiki/Book_of_Revelation "Book of Revelation"). As a [Quaker](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), Penn had experienced [religious persecution](/wiki/Religious_persecution "Religious persecution") and wanted his colony to be a place where anyone could worship freely. This tolerance, which exceeded that of other colonies, led to better relations with the local native tribes and fostered Philadelphia's rapid growth into America's most important city.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Penn planned a city on the Delaware River to serve as a port and place for government. Hoping that Philadelphia would become more like an English rural town instead of a city, Penn laid out roads on a [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan") to keep houses and businesses spread far apart with areas for gardens and [orchards](/wiki/Orchard "Orchard").

The city's inhabitants did not follow Penn's plans, however, and instead crowded the present-day [Port of Philadelphia](/wiki/Port_of_Philadelphia "Port of Philadelphia") on the Delaware River and subdivided and resold their lots.[[50]](#cite_note-51) Before Penn left Philadelphia for the final time, he issued the Charter of 1701 establishing it as a city. Though poor at first, Philadelphia became an important trading center with tolerable living conditions by the 1750s. [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin"), a leading citizen, helped improve city services and founded new ones that were among the first in the nation, including a [fire company](/wiki/Union_Fire_Company "Union Fire Company"), [library](/wiki/Library_Company_of_Philadelphia "Library Company of Philadelphia"), and [hospital](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Hospital "Pennsylvania Hospital").

A number of [philosophical societies](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") were formed, which were centers of the city's intellectual life, including the Philadelphia Society for Promoting Agriculture (1785), the Pennsylvania Society for the Encouragement of Manufactures and the Useful Arts (1787), the [Academy of Natural Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Natural_Sciences_of_Drexel_University "Academy of Natural Sciences of Drexel University") (1812), and the [Franklin Institute](/wiki/Franklin_Institute "Franklin Institute") (1824).[[51]](#cite_note-52) These societies developed and financed new industries that attracted skilled and knowledgeable immigrants from Europe.

### American Revolution

See also: [Pennsylvania in the American Revolution](/wiki/Pennsylvania_in_the_American_Revolution "Pennsylvania in the American Revolution") and [Philadelphia campaign](/wiki/Philadelphia_campaign "Philadelphia campaign")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Declaration_of_Independence_%281819%29%2C_by_John_Trumbull.jpg/250px-Declaration_of_Independence_%281819%29%2C_by_John_Trumbull.jpg)](/wiki/File:Declaration_of_Independence_(1819),_by_John_Trumbull.jpg)

The [Committee of Five](/wiki/Committee_of_Five "Committee of Five") presenting their draft of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") in [Independence Hall](/wiki/Independence_Hall "Independence Hall") on June 28, 1776, depicted in [an 1818 painting](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_(painting) "Declaration of Independence (painting)") by [John Trumbull](/wiki/John_Trumbull "John Trumbull"); historian [Joseph Ellis](/wiki/Joseph_Ellis "Joseph Ellis") called the Declaration "the most potent and consequential words in American history."[[52]](#cite_note-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Philadelphia_Presidents_house.jpg/250px-Philadelphia_Presidents_house.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philadelphia_Presidents_house.jpg)

[President's House](/wiki/President%27s_House_(Philadelphia) "President's House (Philadelphia)") on [Market Street](/wiki/Market_Street_(Philadelphia) "Market Street (Philadelphia)") served as the presidential mansion for the nation's first two presidents, [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") and [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams"), from 1790 to 1800 prior to the completion of the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") and the development of Washington, D.C. as the nation's new capital.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Exterior_of_the_Independence_Hall%2C_Aug_2019.jpg/250px-Exterior_of_the_Independence_Hall%2C_Aug_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exterior_of_the_Independence_Hall,_Aug_2019.jpg)

[Independence Hall](/wiki/Independence_Hall "Independence Hall") on [Chestnut Street](/wiki/Chestnut_Street_(Philadelphia) "Chestnut Street (Philadelphia)") between 5th and 6th streets, where the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") was signed and the [Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") was ratified, on July 4, 1776, and June 21, 1788, respectively

Philadelphia's importance and central location in the colonies made it a natural center for [America's revolutionaries](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"). By the 1750s, Philadelphia surpassed [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") as the largest city and busiest port in [British America](/wiki/British_America "British America"), and the second-largest city in the entire [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") after London.[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55) In 1774, as resentment of the [British government's](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") policies towards the colonies and support for independence began burgeoning in the colonies, Philadelphia hosted the [First Continental Congress](/wiki/First_Continental_Congress "First Continental Congress") at [Carpenters' Hall](/wiki/Carpenters%27_Hall "Carpenters' Hall"), and 12 of the original 13 colonies sent delegates to the Congress.

From 1775 to 1781, Philadelphia hosted the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress"),[[55]](#cite_note-56) whose 56 delegates unanimously adopted the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") inside what was then called Pennsylvania State House and was later renamed [Independence Hall](/wiki/Independence_Hall "Independence Hall"). Written predominantly by [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") from his second-floor apartment on [Market Street](/wiki/Market_Street_(Philadelphia) "Market Street (Philadelphia)") within walking distance of Independence Hall, the Declaration has been described by [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_History "Pulitzer Prize for History")-winning historian [Joseph Ellis](/wiki/Joseph_Ellis "Joseph Ellis") as "the most potent and consequential words in American history,"[[16]](#cite_note-Ellis-2007-16) and its adoption represented a declaration of war against [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"). Since the Declaration's July 4, 1776, adoption, its signing has been cited globally and repeatedly by various peoples of the world seeking independence and liberty. It also has been, since its adoption, the basis for annual celebration by Americans; in 1938, this celebration of the Declaration was formalized as [Independence Day](/wiki/Independence_Day_(United_States) "Independence Day (United States)"), one of only [eleven designated U.S. federal holidays](/wiki/Federal_holidays_in_the_United_States "Federal holidays in the United States").

After [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington")'s defeat at the [Battle of Brandywine](/wiki/Battle_of_Brandywine "Battle of Brandywine") in [Chadds Ford Township](/wiki/Chadds_Ford_Township,_Pennsylvania "Chadds Ford Township, Pennsylvania"), on September 11, 1777, during the [Philadelphia campaign](/wiki/Philadelphia_campaign "Philadelphia campaign"), the revolutionary capital of Philadelphia was defenseless, and the city prepared for what was perceived to be an inevitable British attack. Because bells could easily be recast into munitions, the [Liberty Bell](/wiki/Liberty_Bell "Liberty Bell"), then known as the Pennsylvania State Bell, and bells from two Philadelphia churches, [Christ Church](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Philadelphia "Christ Church, Philadelphia") and [St. Peter's Church](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Episcopal_Church_(Philadelphia) "St. Peter's Episcopal Church (Philadelphia)"), were hastily taken down and transported by heavily guarded wagon train out of the city. The Liberty Bell was taken to [Zion German Reformed Church](/wiki/High_German_Evangelical_Reformed_Church "High German Evangelical Reformed Church") in Northampton Town, which is present-day [Allentown](/wiki/Allentown,_Pennsylvania "Allentown, Pennsylvania"), where it was hidden under the church's floor boards for nine months from September 1777 until departure of British forces from Philadelphia in June 1778.[[56]](#cite_note-57) Two Revolutionary War battles, the [Siege of Fort Mifflin](/wiki/Siege_of_Fort_Mifflin "Siege of Fort Mifflin"), fought between September 26 and November 16, 1777, and the [Battle of Germantown](/wiki/Battle_of_Germantown "Battle of Germantown"), fought on October 4, 1777, took place within Philadelphia's city limits.

In Philadelphia, the Second Continental Congress adopted the [Articles of Confederation](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation "Articles of Confederation") on November 15, 1777. Independence Hall in Philadelphia was the meeting place for the [Constitutional Convention](/wiki/Constitutional_Convention_(United_States) "Constitutional Convention (United States)"), which ratified the [Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") on September 17, 1787, which is now the longest-standing codified national constitution.

Philadelphia served as capital of the United States for most of the colonial and early post-colonial period, including for a decade, from 1790 to 1800, while Washington, D.C. was being constructed and prepared to serve as the new national capital, and [on five prior occasions](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") between 1776 and 1790.[[57]](#cite_note-58) In 1793, the largest [yellow fever epidemic](/wiki/1793_Philadelphia_yellow_fever_epidemic "1793 Philadelphia yellow fever epidemic") in U.S. history killed approximately 4,000 to 5,000 people in Philadelphia, or about ten percent of the city's population at the time.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60) The capital of the United States was moved to Washington, D.C. in 1800 upon completion of the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") and [U.S. Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol") buildings.

The state capital was moved from Philadelphia to [Lancaster](/wiki/Lancaster,_Pennsylvania "Lancaster, Pennsylvania") in 1799, then ultimately to [Harrisburg](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Pennsylvania "Harrisburg, Pennsylvania") in 1812. Philadelphia remained the nation's largest city until the late 18th century. It also was the nation's financial and cultural center until ultimately being eclipsed in total population by [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") in 1790. In 1816, the city's free Black community founded the [African Methodist Episcopal Church](/wiki/African_Methodist_Episcopal_Church "African Methodist Episcopal Church"), the first independent Black denomination in the country, and the first Black [Episcopal Church](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"). The free Black community also established many schools for its children with the help of [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"). Large-scale construction projects for new roads, [canals](/wiki/Canal "Canal"), and railroads made Philadelphia the first major [industrial](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") city in the United States.

### 19th century

Further information: [Pennsylvania in the American Civil War](/wiki/Pennsylvania_in_the_American_Civil_War "Pennsylvania in the American Civil War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Centennial_Exhibition%2C_Opening_Day.jpg/250px-Centennial_Exhibition%2C_Opening_Day.jpg)](/wiki/File:Centennial_Exhibition,_Opening_Day.jpg)

Opening day ceremonies at the [Centennial Exposition](/wiki/Centennial_Exposition "Centennial Exposition") at [Memorial Hall](/wiki/Memorial_Hall_(Philadelphia) "Memorial Hall (Philadelphia)") in [Fairmount Park](/wiki/Fairmount_Park "Fairmount Park") in 1876, the first [world fair](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair") held in the U.S. on the centennial anniversary of the nation's founding

Throughout the 19th century, Philadelphia hosted a variety of industries and businesses; the largest was the [textile industry](/wiki/Textile_industry "Textile industry"). Major corporations in the 19th and early 20th centuries included the [Baldwin Locomotive Works](/wiki/Baldwin_Locomotive_Works "Baldwin Locomotive Works"), [William Cramp & Sons Shipbuilding Company](/wiki/William_Cramp_%26_Sons "William Cramp & Sons"), and the [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad").[[60]](#cite_note-61) Established in 1870, the Philadelphia Conveyancers' Association was chartered by the state in 1871. Along with the U.S. Centennial in 1876, the city's industry was celebrated in the [Centennial Exposition](/wiki/Centennial_Exposition "Centennial Exposition"), the first official [World's fair](/wiki/World%27s_fair "World's fair") in the U.S.

Immigrants, mostly from Ireland and Germany, settled in Philadelphia and the surrounding districts. These immigrants were largely responsible for the [first general strike in North America](/wiki/1835_Philadelphia_general_strike "1835 Philadelphia general strike") in 1835, in which workers in the city won the ten-hour workday. The city was a destination for thousands of Irish immigrants fleeing the [Great Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") in the 1840s; housing for them was developed south of [South Street](/wiki/South_Street_(Philadelphia) "South Street (Philadelphia)") and later occupied by succeeding immigrants. They established a network of [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") churches and schools and dominated the Catholic clergy for decades. Anti-Irish, anti-Catholic [nativist](/wiki/Nativism_(politics) "Nativism (politics)") [riots](/wiki/Philadelphia_nativist_riots "Philadelphia nativist riots") erupted in Philadelphia in 1844. The rise in population of the surrounding districts helped lead to the [Act of Consolidation of 1854](/wiki/Act_of_Consolidation,_1854 "Act of Consolidation, 1854"), which extended the city limits from the 2 square miles (5.2 km2) of [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia") to the roughly 134 square miles (350 km2) of [Philadelphia County](/wiki/Philadelphia_County,_Pennsylvania "Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania").[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[62]](#cite_note-Consolidation_Act_of_1854-63)
In the latter half of the 19th century and leading into the 20th century, immigrants from Russia, Eastern Europe, and Italy, and African Americans from the [southern U.S.](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") settled in the city.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

Philadelphia was represented by the [Washington Grays](/wiki/Washington_Grays_(Philadelphia) "Washington Grays (Philadelphia)") in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). The African-American population of Philadelphia increased from 31,699 to 219,559 between 1880 and 1930, largely stemming from the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") from the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States").[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66)

### 20th century

By the 20th century, Philadelphia had an entrenched [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") [political machine](/wiki/Political_machine "Political machine") and a complacent population.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*][[66]](#cite_note-67) In 1910, [a general strike](/wiki/Philadelphia_general_strike_(1910) "Philadelphia general strike (1910)") shut down the entire city.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

In 1917, following outrage over the election-year murder of a Philadelphia police officer, the [City Council](/wiki/Philadelphia_City_Council "Philadelphia City Council") shrank from two houses to just one.[[68]](#cite_note-69) In July 1919, Philadelphia was one of more than 36 industrial cities nationally to suffer a [race riot](/wiki/Race_riots_in_Philadelphia_during_the_1919_Red_Summer "Race riots in Philadelphia during the 1919 Red Summer") during [Red Summer](/wiki/Red_Summer "Red Summer") in post-[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") unrest as recent immigrants competed with Black residents for jobs. In the 1920s, the public flouting of [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") laws, [organized crime](/wiki/Organized_crime "Organized crime"), mob violence, and corrupt police involvement in illegal activities led to the appointment of [Brig. Gen.](/wiki/Brigadier_general "Brigadier general") [Smedley Butler](/wiki/Smedley_Butler "Smedley Butler") of the [U.S. Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") as the city's director of public safety, but political pressure still prevented long-term success in fighting crime and corruption.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

In 1940, [non-Hispanic whites](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_whites "Non-Hispanic whites") constituted 86.8% of the city's population.[[70]](#cite_note-71) In 1950, the population peaked at more than two million residents, then began to decline with the restructuring of industry that led to the loss of many middle-class union jobs. In addition, suburbanization enticed many affluent residents to depart the city for its outlying railroad commuting towns and newer housing. The resulting reduction in Philadelphia's tax base and the resources of local government caused the city to struggle through a long period of adjustment, and it approached bankruptcy by the late 1980s.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

In 1985, the [Philadelphia Police Department](/wiki/Philadelphia_Police_Department "Philadelphia Police Department"), utilizing a [Pennsylvania State Police](/wiki/Pennsylvania_State_Police "Pennsylvania State Police") helicopter, [bombed](/wiki/Aerial_bombing "Aerial bombing") the [Cobbs Creek](/wiki/Cobbs_Creek,_Philadelphia "Cobbs Creek, Philadelphia") neighborhood to execute arrest warrants on [MOVE](/wiki/MOVE_(Philadelphia_organization) "MOVE (Philadelphia organization)") members, a [Black liberation](/wiki/Black_power_movement "Black power movement") movement. [The incident](/wiki/1985_MOVE_bombing "1985 MOVE bombing") killed 11 people, destroyed 61 homes, and displaced 250 residents, marking one of the only times a US city intentionally bombed its own civilians.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

Revitalization and [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") of neighborhoods began in the late 1970s and continues into the 21st century with much of the development occurring in the [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia") and [University City](/wiki/University_City,_Philadelphia,_Pennsylvania "University City, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania") neighborhoods. But this expanded a shortage of [affordable housing](/wiki/Affordable_housing "Affordable housing") in the city. After many manufacturers and businesses left Philadelphia or shut down, the city started attracting service businesses and began to market itself more aggressively as a tourist destination. Contemporary glass-and-granite [skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Philadelphia "List of tallest buildings in Philadelphia") were built in Center City beginning in the 1980s. Historic areas such as [Old City](/wiki/Old_City,_Philadelphia "Old City, Philadelphia") and [Society Hill](/wiki/Society_Hill "Society Hill") were renovated during the reformist mayoral era of the 1950s through the 1980s, making both areas among the most desirable Center City neighborhoods. Immigrants from around the world began to enter the U.S. through Philadelphia as their gateway, leading to a reversal of the city's population decline between 1950 and 2000, during which it lost about 25 percent of its residents.[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)

### 21st century

Philadelphia eventually began experiencing a growth in its population in 2007, which continued with incremental annual increases through the present.[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-United_States_Census_Bureau-78) A migration pattern has been established from New York City to Philadelphia by residents opting for a large city with relative proximity and a lower [cost of living](/wiki/Cost_of_living "Cost of living").[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80)

## Geography

### Topography

Philadelphia's geographic center is about 40° 0′ 34″ north latitude and 75° 8′ 0″ west longitude. The [40th parallel north](/wiki/40th_parallel_north "40th parallel north") passes through neighborhoods in [Northeast Philadelphia](/wiki/Northeast_Philadelphia "Northeast Philadelphia"), [North Philadelphia](/wiki/North_Philadelphia "North Philadelphia"), and [West Philadelphia](/wiki/West_Philadelphia "West Philadelphia") including [Fairmount Park](/wiki/Fairmount_Park "Fairmount Park"). The city encompasses 142.71 square miles (369.62 km2), of which 134.18 square miles (347.52 km2) is land and 8.53 square miles (22.09 km2), or 6%, is water.[[80]](#cite_note-81) Natural bodies of water include the [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") and [Schuylkill](/wiki/Schuylkill_River "Schuylkill River") rivers, lakes in [Franklin Delano Roosevelt Park](/wiki/Franklin_Delano_Roosevelt_Park "Franklin Delano Roosevelt Park"), and [Cobbs](/wiki/Cobbs_Creek "Cobbs Creek"), [Wissahickon](/wiki/Wissahickon_Creek "Wissahickon Creek"), and [Pennypack](/wiki/Pennypack_Creek "Pennypack Creek") creeks. The largest artificial body of water is East Park Reservoir in [Fairmount Park](/wiki/Fairmount_Park "Fairmount Park").

The lowest point is sea level and the highest point is in [Chestnut Hill](/wiki/Chestnut_Hill,_Philadelphia "Chestnut Hill, Philadelphia"), about 446 feet (136 m) above sea level on Summit Street near the intersection of Germantown Avenue and [Bethlehem Pike](/wiki/Bethlehem_Pike "Bethlehem Pike") at: 40.07815 N, 75.20747 W.[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83) Philadelphia is located on the [Atlantic Seaboard Fall Line](/wiki/Atlantic_Seaboard_Fall_Line "Atlantic Seaboard Fall Line") that separates the [Atlantic Plain](/wiki/Atlantic_Plain "Atlantic Plain") from the [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont_(United_States) "Piedmont (United States)").[[83]](#cite_note-84) The Schuylkill River's rapids at [East Falls](/wiki/East_Falls,_Philadelphia "East Falls, Philadelphia") were inundated by completion of the dam at [Fairmount Water Works](/wiki/Fairmount_Water_Works "Fairmount Water Works").[[84]](#cite_note-85)

The city is the seat of [its own county](/wiki/Philadelphia_County,_Pennsylvania "Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania"). The city is bordered by six adjacent counties: [Montgomery](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "Montgomery County, Pennsylvania") to the northwest; [Bucks](/wiki/Bucks_County,_Pennsylvania "Bucks County, Pennsylvania") to the north and northeast; [Burlington County, New Jersey](/wiki/Burlington_County,_New_Jersey "Burlington County, New Jersey") to the east; [Camden County, New Jersey](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey") to the southeast; [Gloucester County, New Jersey](/wiki/Gloucester_County,_New_Jersey "Gloucester County, New Jersey") to the south; and [Delaware County](/wiki/Delaware_County,_Pennsylvania "Delaware County, Pennsylvania") to the southwest.

#### City planning

See also: [List of Philadelphia neighborhoods](/wiki/List_of_Philadelphia_neighborhoods "List of Philadelphia neighborhoods")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/A651%2C_Philadelphia_skyline_from_the_Spring_Garden_Street_Bridge%2C_2018.jpg/960px-A651%2C_Philadelphia_skyline_from_the_Spring_Garden_Street_Bridge%2C_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:A651,_Philadelphia_skyline_from_the_Spring_Garden_Street_Bridge,_2018.jpg "The skyline of Philadelphia seen from the northwest on Spring Garden Street Bridge over the Schuylkill River in April 2018 (annotated version)")

The skyline of Philadelphia seen from the northwest on [Spring Garden Street Bridge](/wiki/Spring_Garden_Street_Bridge "Spring Garden Street Bridge") over the [Schuylkill River](/wiki/Schuylkill_River "Schuylkill River") in April 2018 (annotated version)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Philadelphia_from_South_Street_Bridge_July_2016_panorama_3b.jpg/960px-Philadelphia_from_South_Street_Bridge_July_2016_panorama_3b.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philadelphia_from_South_Street_Bridge_July_2016_panorama_3b.jpg "Philadelphia's skyline at twilight from the southwest on South Street Bridge with the Schuylkill River on the left in July 2016 (annotated version)")

Philadelphia's skyline at twilight from the southwest on [South Street Bridge](/wiki/South_Street_Bridge_(Philadelphia,_Pennsylvania) "South Street Bridge (Philadelphia, Pennsylvania)") with the [Schuylkill River](/wiki/Schuylkill_River "Schuylkill River") on the left in July 2016 (annotated version)

Philadelphia was created in the 17th century, following the plan by [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn")'s surveyor [Thomas Holme](/wiki/Thomas_Holme "Thomas Holme"). [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia") is structured with long, straight streets running nearly due east–west and north–south, forming a grid pattern between the [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") and [Schuylkill](/wiki/Schuylkill_River "Schuylkill River") rivers that is aligned with their courses. The original city plan was designed to allow for easy travel and to keep residences separated by open space that would help prevent the spread of fire.[[85]](#cite_note-Daly,_Molly-2011-86) In keeping with the idea of a "Greene Countrie Towne", and inspired by the many types of trees that grew in the region, Penn named many of the east–west streets for local trees.[[86]](#cite_note-87) Penn planned the creation of five public parks in the city which were renamed in 1824.[[85]](#cite_note-Daly,_Molly-2011-86) Centre Square was renamed [Penn Square](/wiki/Penn_Square,_Philadelphia "Penn Square, Philadelphia");[[87]](#cite_note-88) Northeast Square was renamed [Franklin Square](/wiki/Franklin_Square_(Philadelphia) "Franklin Square (Philadelphia)"); Southeast Square was renamed [Washington Square](/wiki/Washington_Square_(Philadelphia) "Washington Square (Philadelphia)"); Southwest Square was renamed [Rittenhouse Square](/wiki/Rittenhouse_Square "Rittenhouse Square"); and Northwest Square was renamed [Logan Circle/Square](/wiki/Logan_Circle_(Philadelphia) "Logan Circle (Philadelphia)").[[88]](#cite_note-89) [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia") had an estimated 183,240 residents as of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philadelphia&action=edit), making it the second-most populated downtown area in the United States after [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") in New York City.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

Philadelphia's neighborhoods are divided into six large sections that surround Center City: [North Philadelphia](/wiki/North_Philadelphia "North Philadelphia"), [Northeast Philadelphia](/wiki/Northeast_Philadelphia "Northeast Philadelphia"), [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia"), [Southwest Philadelphia](/wiki/Southwest_Philadelphia "Southwest Philadelphia"), [West Philadelphia](/wiki/West_Philadelphia "West Philadelphia"), and [Northwest Philadelphia](/wiki/Northwest_Philadelphia "Northwest Philadelphia"). The city's geographic boundaries have been largely unchanged since these neighborhoods were consolidated in 1854. However, each of these large areas contains numerous neighborhoods, some of whose boundaries derive from the boroughs, townships, and other communities that constituted [Pennsylvania County](/wiki/Philadelphia_County,_Pennsylvania "Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania") before their inclusion within the city.[[90]](#cite_note-91)

The [City Planning Commission](/wiki/City_Planning_Commission_(Philadelphia) "City Planning Commission (Philadelphia)"), tasked with guiding growth and development of the city, has divided the city into 18 planning districts as part of the Philadelphia2035 physical development plan.[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93) Much of the city's 1980 zoning code was overhauled from 2007 to 2012 as part of a joint effort between former mayors [John F. Street](/wiki/John_F._Street "John F. Street") and [Michael Nutter](/wiki/Michael_Nutter "Michael Nutter"). The zoning changes were intended to rectify incorrect zoning maps to facilitate future community development, as the city forecasts an additional 100,000 residents and 40,000 jobs will be added by 2035.

The [Philadelphia Housing Authority](/wiki/Philadelphia_Housing_Authority "Philadelphia Housing Authority") (PHA) is the largest landlord in Pennsylvania. Established in 1937, the PHA is the nation's fourth-largest housing authority, serving about 81,000 people with affordable housing, while employing 1,400 on a budget of $371 million.[[93]](#cite_note-94) The [Philadelphia Parking Authority](/wiki/Philadelphia_Parking_Authority "Philadelphia Parking Authority") is responsible for ensuring adequate parking for city residents, businesses, and visitors.[[94]](#cite_note-95)

#### Architecture

Main articles: [Architecture of Philadelphia](/wiki/Architecture_of_Philadelphia "Architecture of Philadelphia") and [List of tallest buildings in Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Philadelphia "List of tallest buildings in Philadelphia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/OneLiberyPlacePhiladelphia_cropped.jpg/250px-OneLiberyPlacePhiladelphia_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:OneLiberyPlacePhiladelphia_cropped.jpg)

Two of [Center City Philadelphia's](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia") most prominent high-rise buildings, [One Liberty Place](/wiki/Liberty_Place#One_Liberty_Place "Liberty Place"), built between 1985 and 1987 (in background), and [Philadelphia City Hall](/wiki/Philadelphia_City_Hall "Philadelphia City Hall"), built between 1871 and 1901 (in foreground)

Philadelphia's architectural history dates back to [colonial](/wiki/Colonial_history_of_the_United_States "Colonial history of the United States") times and includes a wide range of styles. The earliest structures were constructed with [logs](/wiki/Log_house "Log house"), but brick structures were common by 1700. During the 18th century, the [cityscape](/wiki/Cityscape "Cityscape") was dominated by [Georgian architecture](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture"), including [Independence Hall](/wiki/Independence_Hall "Independence Hall") and [Christ Church](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Philadelphia "Christ Church, Philadelphia").

In the first decades of the 19th century, [Federal](/wiki/Federal_architecture "Federal architecture") and [Greek Revival architecture](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture") were the dominant styles produced by Philadelphia architects such as [Benjamin Latrobe](/wiki/Benjamin_Henry_Latrobe "Benjamin Henry Latrobe"), [William Strickland](/wiki/William_Strickland_(architect) "William Strickland (architect)"), [John Haviland](/wiki/John_Haviland "John Haviland"), [John Notman](/wiki/John_Notman "John Notman"), [Thomas Walter](/wiki/Thomas_Ustick_Walter "Thomas Ustick Walter"), and [Samuel Sloan](/wiki/Samuel_Sloan_(architect) "Samuel Sloan (architect)").[[95]](#cite_note-Philadelphia-96) [Frank Furness](/wiki/Frank_Furness "Frank Furness") is considered Philadelphia's greatest architect of the second half of the 19th century. His contemporaries included [John McArthur Jr.](/wiki/John_McArthur_Jr. "John McArthur Jr."), [Addison Hutton](/wiki/Addison_Hutton "Addison Hutton"), [Wilson Eyre](/wiki/Wilson_Eyre "Wilson Eyre"), the [Wilson Brothers](/wiki/Wilson_Brothers_%26_Company "Wilson Brothers & Company"), and [Horace Trumbauer](/wiki/Horace_Trumbauer "Horace Trumbauer"). In 1871, construction began on the [Second Empire](/wiki/Second_Empire_architecture_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Second Empire architecture in the United States and Canada")-style [Philadelphia City Hall](/wiki/Philadelphia_City_Hall "Philadelphia City Hall"). The [Philadelphia Historical Commission](/wiki/Philadelphia_Historical_Commission "Philadelphia Historical Commission") was created in 1955 to preserve the cultural and architectural history of the city. The commission maintains the [Philadelphia Register of Historic Places](/wiki/Philadelphia_Register_of_Historic_Places "Philadelphia Register of Historic Places"), adding historic buildings, structures, sites, objects and districts as it sees fit.[[96]](#cite_note-97)

In 1932, Philadelphia became home to the first modern [International Style](/wiki/International_Style_(architecture) "International Style (architecture)") skyscraper in the United States, the [PSFS Building](/wiki/Loews_Philadelphia_Hotel "Loews Philadelphia Hotel"), designed by [George Howe](/wiki/George_Howe_(architect) "George Howe (architect)") and [William Lescaze](/wiki/William_Lescaze "William Lescaze"). The 548 ft (167 m) City Hall remained the tallest building in the city until 1987 when [One Liberty Place](/wiki/Liberty_Place#One_Liberty_Place "Liberty Place") was completed. Numerous glass and granite skyscrapers were built in Center City beginning in the late 1980s. In 2007, the [Comcast Center](/wiki/Comcast_Center "Comcast Center") surpassed One Liberty Place to become the city's tallest building. The [Comcast Technology Center](/wiki/Comcast_Technology_Center "Comcast Technology Center") was completed in 2018, reaching a height of 1,121 ft (342 m), as the [tallest building in the United States](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_United_States "List of tallest buildings in the United States") outside of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and Chicago.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

For much of Philadelphia's history, the typical home has been the [row house](/wiki/Terraced_house "Terraced house"). The row house was introduced to the United States via Philadelphia in the early 19th century and, for a time, row houses built elsewhere in the United States were known as "Philadelphia rows".[[95]](#cite_note-Philadelphia-96) A variety of row houses are found throughout the city, from Federal-style continuous blocks in [Old City](/wiki/Old_City,_Philadelphia "Old City, Philadelphia") and [Society Hill](/wiki/Society_Hill "Society Hill") to Victorian-style homes in [North Philadelphia](/wiki/North_Philadelphia "North Philadelphia") to twin row houses in [West Philadelphia](/wiki/West_Philadelphia "West Philadelphia"). While newer homes have been built recently, much of the housing dates to the 18th, 19th, and early 20th centuries, which has created problems such as [urban decay](/wiki/Urban_decay "Urban decay") and vacant lots. Some neighborhoods, including [Northern Liberties](/wiki/Northern_Liberties,_Philadelphia "Northern Liberties, Philadelphia") and Society Hill, have been rehabilitated through [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification").[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100)

#### Parks

See also: [Drinking fountains in Philadelphia](/wiki/Drinking_fountains_in_Philadelphia "Drinking fountains in Philadelphia"), [Fairmount Park](/wiki/Fairmount_Park "Fairmount Park"), and [List of parks in Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_parks_in_Philadelphia "List of parks in Philadelphia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Detroit_Photographic_Company_%280757%29.jpg/250px-Detroit_Photographic_Company_%280757%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Detroit_Photographic_Company_(0757).jpg)

Philadelphia's [Fairmount Park](/wiki/Fairmount_Park "Fairmount Park") on the [Schuylkill River](/wiki/Schuylkill_River "Schuylkill River"), the city's largest and one of the world's largest public parks

As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philadelphia&action=edit), the city's total park space, including municipal, state, and federal parks in the city, amounts to 11,211 acres (17.5 sq mi).[[22]](#cite_note-tpl.org-22) Philadelphia's largest park is [Fairmount Park](/wiki/Fairmount_Park "Fairmount Park"), which includes the [Philadelphia Zoo](/wiki/Philadelphia_Zoo "Philadelphia Zoo") and encompasses 2,052 acres (3.2 sq mi) of the total parkland. Fairmount Park's adjacent [Wissahickon Valley Park](/wiki/Wissahickon_Valley_Park "Wissahickon Valley Park") contains 2,042 acres (3.2 sq mi).[[101]](#cite_note-102) Fairmount Park, when combined with Wissahickon Valley Park, is one of the largest contiguous [urban park](/wiki/Urban_park "Urban park") areas in the U.S.[[22]](#cite_note-tpl.org-22) The two parks, along with the [Colonial Revival](/wiki/Colonial_Revival_architecture "Colonial Revival architecture"), [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") and [Federal](/wiki/Federal_architecture "Federal architecture")-style [mansions](/wiki/List_of_houses_in_Fairmount_Park "List of houses in Fairmount Park") in them, have been listed as one entity on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") since 1972.[[102]](#cite_note-103)

Within the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), Philadelphia falls under the northern periphery of the [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") zone (Köppen *Cfa*),[[103]](#cite_note-104) while under the [Trewartha climate classification](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification"), the city is classified as a [maritime climate](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate") (*Do*) bordered to the north by the [continental](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") (*Dc*) zone.[[104]](#cite_note-105) Summers are typically hot and muggy. Fall and spring are generally mild, and winter is moderately cold. The plant life [hardiness zones](/wiki/Hardiness_zone#Selected_U.S._cities "Hardiness zone") are 7a and 7b, reflecting an average annual extreme minimum temperature between 0 and 10 °F (−18 and −12 °C).[[105]](#cite_note-106)

Snowfall is highly variable. Some winters have only light snow while others include major snowstorms. The normal seasonal snowfall averages 22.4 in (57 cm), with rare snowfalls in November or April, and rarely any sustained snow cover.[[106]](#cite_note-National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration-2-107) Seasonal snowfall accumulation has ranged from trace amounts in 1972–73, to 78.7 inches (200 cm) in the winter of 2009–10.[[106]](#cite_note-National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration-2-107)[[b]](#cite_note-108) The city's [heaviest single-storm snowfall](/wiki/North_American_blizzard_of_1996#Philadelphia_and_Pennsylvania "North American blizzard of 1996") was 30.7 in (78 cm), which occurred in January 1996.[[107]](#cite_note-109)

Precipitation is generally spread throughout the year, with eight to eleven wet days per month,[[108]](#cite_note-110) at an average annual rate of 44.1 inches (1,120 mm), but historically ranging from 29.31 in (744 mm) in 1922 to 64.33 in (1,634 mm) in 2011.[[106]](#cite_note-National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration-2-107) The most rain recorded in one day occurred on July 28, 2013, when 8.02 in (204 mm) fell at [Philadelphia International Airport](/wiki/Philadelphia_International_Airport "Philadelphia International Airport").[[106]](#cite_note-National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration-2-107) Philadelphia has a moderately sunny climate with an average of 2,498 [hours of sunshine](/wiki/Sunshine_duration "Sunshine duration") annually. The percentage of sunshine ranges from 47% in December to 61% in June, July, and August.[[109]](#cite_note-National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration-111)

The January daily average temperature is 33.7 °F (0.9 °C). The temperature frequently rises to 50 °F (10 °C) during thaws. July averages 78.7 °F (25.9 °C). Heat waves accompanied by high humidity and [heat indices](/wiki/Heat_index "Heat index") are frequent, with highs reaching or exceeding 90 °F (32 °C) on 30 days of the year. The average window for freezing temperatures is November 6 to April 2,[[106]](#cite_note-National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration-2-107) allowing a growing season of 217 days. Early fall and late winter are generally dry, with February having the lowest average precipitation at 2.75 inches (70 mm). The dewpoint in the summer averages between 59.1 and 64.5 °F (15 and 18 °C).[[106]](#cite_note-National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration-2-107)

The highest recorded temperature was 106 °F (41 °C) on August 7, 1918. Temperatures at or above 100 °F (38 °C) are not common, with the last occurrence of such a temperature being July 21, 2019.[[110]](#cite_note-Philadelphia_Record_Highs_and_Lows-112) The lowest officially recorded temperature was −11 °F (−24 °C) on February 9, 1934.[[110]](#cite_note-Philadelphia_Record_Highs_and_Lows-112) Temperatures at or below 0 °F (−18 °C) are rare, with the last such occurrence being [January 19, 1994](/wiki/1994_North_American_cold_wave "1994 North American cold wave").[[106]](#cite_note-National_Oceanic_and_Atmospheric_Administration-2-107) The record low maximum is 5 °F (−15 °C) on February 10, 1899, and December 30, 1880. The record high minimum is 83 °F (28 °C) on July 23, 2011, and July 24, 2010.[[111]](#cite_note-113) The record high dew point is 82 °F (28 °C) which occurred on July 15, 1995.[[112]](#cite_note-114)

#### Air quality

Philadelphia County received an [ozone](/wiki/Ozone "Ozone") grade of F and a 24-hour [particle pollution](/wiki/Particulates "Particulates") rating of D in the [American Lung Association](/wiki/American_Lung_Association "American Lung Association")'s 2017 State of the Air report, which analyzed data from 2013 to 2015.[[119]](#cite_note-123)[[120]](#cite_note-American_Lung_Association-124) The city was ranked 22nd for ozone, 20th for short-term particle pollution, and 11th for year-round particle pollution.[[121]](#cite_note-American_Lung_Association-2-125) According to the same report, the city experienced a significant reduction in high ozone days since 2001—from nearly 50 days per year to fewer than 10—along with fewer days of high particle pollution since 2000—from about 19 days per year to about 3—and an approximate 30% reduction in annual levels of particle pollution since 2000.[[120]](#cite_note-American_Lung_Association-124)

Five of the ten largest [combined statistical areas](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area#List_of_combined_statistical_areas "Combined statistical area") (CSAs) were ranked higher for ozone: Los Angeles (1st), New York City (9th), [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston") (12th), [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") (13th), and [San Jose, California](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California") (18th). Many smaller CSAs were also ranked higher for ozone, including [Sacramento](/wiki/Sacramento,_California "Sacramento, California") (8th), [Las Vegas](/wiki/Las_Vegas "Las Vegas") (10th), [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver") (11th), [El Paso](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas") (16th), and [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City") (20th). Only two of those same ten CSAs, San Jose and Los Angeles, were ranked higher than Philadelphia for both year-round and short-term particle pollution.[[121]](#cite_note-American_Lung_Association-2-125)

As of the [2020 U.S. census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), there were 1,603,797 people residing in Philadelphia, representing a 1.2% increase from the 2019 census estimate.[[77]](#cite_note-United_States_Census_Bureau-78) The racial composition of the city was 39.3% Black alone (42.0% Black alone or in combination), 36.3% White alone (41.9% White alone or in combination), 8.7% Asian alone, 0.4% American Indian and Alaska Native alone, 8.7% some other race, and 6.9% multiracial. 14.9% of residents were Hispanic or Latino.[[124]](#cite_note-Explore_Census_Data-128)

34.8% had a bachelor's degree or higher. 23.9% spoke a language other than English at home, the most common of which was Spanish (10.8%). 15.0% of the population is foreign born, roughly half of whom are naturalized U.S. citizens. 3.7% of the population are veterans. The median household income was $52,889 and 22.8% of the population lived in poverty. 49.5% of the population drove alone to work, while 23.2% used public transit, 8.2% carpooled, 7.9% walked, and 7.0% worked from home. The average commute is 31 minutes.[[124]](#cite_note-Explore_Census_Data-128)

After the [1950 census](/wiki/1950_United_States_census "1950 United States census"), when a record high of 2,071,605 was recorded, the city's population began a long decline. The population dropped to a low of 1,488,710 residents in 2006 before beginning to rise again. Between 2006 and 2017, Philadelphia added 92,153 residents. In 2017, the U.S. Census Bureau estimated that the racial composition of the city was 41.3% Black (non-Hispanic), 34.9% White (non-Hispanic), 14.1% Hispanic or Latino, 7.1% Asian, 0.4% Native American, 0.05% Pacific Islander, and 2.8% multiracial.[[125]](#cite_note-129)

Ethnic origins in Philadelphia

### Immigration and cultural diversity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Italian_Market_Vegetable_Stand_3000px.jpg/250px-Italian_Market_Vegetable_Stand_3000px.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italian_Market_Vegetable_Stand_3000px.jpg)

Philadelphia's famed [Italian Market](/wiki/Italian_Market,_Philadelphia "Italian Market, Philadelphia") in [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/13th_Gayborhood.jpg/250px-13th_Gayborhood.jpg)](/wiki/File:13th_Gayborhood.jpg)

A [Gayborhood](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_Philadelphia "LGBT culture in Philadelphia") [street sign](/wiki/Rainbow_flag_(LGBT_movement) "Rainbow flag (LGBT movement)") near [Washington Square](/wiki/Washington_Square_West,_Philadelphia "Washington Square West, Philadelphia")

In addition to the city's economic growth, the city's population has been fueled by foreign immigration. According to [The Pew Charitable Trusts](/wiki/The_Pew_Charitable_Trusts "The Pew Charitable Trusts"), the city's [foreign-born](/wiki/Foreign_born "Foreign born") population increased by 69% between 2000 and 2016 to constitute nearly 20% of Philadelphia's workforce,[[129]](#cite_note-134) and it doubled between 1990 and 2017 to constitute 13.8% of the city's total population, with the top five countries of origin being China by a significant margin followed by the Dominican Republic, Jamaica, India, and Vietnam.[[130]](#cite_note-135)

Top 10 countries of origin for foreign-born Philadelphians, 2017[[131]](#cite_note-136)

| Dominican Republic | 13,792 |
| Jamaica | 13,500 |
