Irish, Italian, German, Polish, English, Russian, Ukrainian, and French ancestries constitute the largest [European](/wiki/European_emigration "European emigration") ethnic groups in the city.[[132]](#cite_note-137) Philadelphia has the second-largest Irish and Italian populations in the United States after New York City. [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia") remains one of the largest [Italian](/wiki/Italian-American "Italian-American") neighborhoods in the country and is home to the [Italian Market](/wiki/Italian_Market,_Philadelphia "Italian Market, Philadelphia").

The [Pennsport](/wiki/Pennsport,_Philadelphia "Pennsport, Philadelphia") neighborhood and [Gray's Ferry](/wiki/Grays_Ferry,_Philadelphia "Grays Ferry, Philadelphia") section of South Philadelphia, home to many [Mummer](/wiki/Mummers_Parade "Mummers Parade") clubs, are well known as [Irish](/wiki/Irish-American "Irish-American") neighborhoods. The [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington,_Philadelphia "Kensington, Philadelphia"), [Port Richmond](/wiki/Port_Richmond,_Philadelphia "Port Richmond, Philadelphia"), and [Fishtown](/wiki/Fishtown,_Philadelphia "Fishtown, Philadelphia") neighborhoods have historically been heavily Irish and Polish. Port Richmond is a center for the Polish-American community in Philadelphia, and it remains a common destination for Polish immigrants. [Northeast Philadelphia](/wiki/Northeast_Philadelphia "Northeast Philadelphia"), although known for its Irish and Irish-American population, is home to a Jewish and Russian population. [Mount Airy](/wiki/Mount_Airy,_Philadelphia "Mount Airy, Philadelphia") in [Northwest Philadelphia](/wiki/Northwest_Philadelphia "Northwest Philadelphia") also contains a Jewish community. Nearby [Chestnut Hill](/wiki/Chestnut_Hill,_Philadelphia "Chestnut Hill, Philadelphia") is historically known as an [Anglo-Saxon Protestant](/wiki/White_Anglo-Saxon_Protestant "White Anglo-Saxon Protestant") community.

Philadelphia's [Black American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") population is the fourth-largest in the country after New York City, Chicago, and [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"). [West Philadelphia](/wiki/West_Philadelphia "West Philadelphia") and [North Philadelphia](/wiki/North_Philadelphia "North Philadelphia") are largely African-American neighborhoods, but many are leaving those areas in favor of the Northeast and Southwest sections of Philadelphia. A higher proportion of [African-American Muslims](/wiki/African-American_Muslims "African-American Muslims") reside in Philadelphia than most other major U.S. cities. West Philadelphia and [Southwest Philadelphia](/wiki/Southwest_Philadelphia "Southwest Philadelphia") are home to various [Afro-Caribbean](/wiki/West_Indian_Americans "West Indian Americans") and [African immigrant](/wiki/African_immigration_to_the_United_States "African immigration to the United States") communities.[[133]](#cite_note-138)

The [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans_in_Philadelphia "Puerto Ricans in Philadelphia") population in Philadelphia is the second-largest on the U.S. mainland after New York City, and the second-fastest growing after [Orlando](/wiki/Orlando,_Florida "Orlando, Florida").[[134]](#cite_note-139) Eastern North Philadelphia, particularly [Fairhill](/wiki/Fairhill,_Philadelphia "Fairhill, Philadelphia") and surrounding areas to the north and east, has one of the highest concentrations of Puerto Ricans outside Puerto Rico, with many large swaths of blocks being close to 100% Puerto Rican.[[135]](#cite_note-140)[[136]](#cite_note-141) Puerto Rican and [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Americans "Dominican Americans") populations reside in [North Philadelphia](/wiki/North_Philadelphia "North Philadelphia") and the Northeast, and [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_American "Mexican American") and Central American populations exist in South Philadelphia.[[137]](#cite_note-142) South American migrants were being transported by bus from [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") to Philadelphia beginning in 2022.[[138]](#cite_note-143)

Philadelphia's [Asian American](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") population includes those of Chinese, Indians, Vietnamese, South Koreans, Filipinos, Cambodians, and Indonesians. Over 35,000 Chinese Americans lived in the city in 2015,[[139]](#cite_note-144) including a [Fuzhounese](/wiki/Fuzhounese_Americans "Fuzhounese Americans") population. Center City hosts a [Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Philadelphia "Chinatown, Philadelphia") that is served by [Chinatown bus lines](/wiki/Chinatown_bus_lines "Chinatown bus lines") with service to/from [Chinatown, Manhattan](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan").[[140]](#cite_note-145) [Indians](/wiki/Indians_in_the_Philadelphia_metropolitan_area "Indians in the Philadelphia metropolitan area") make up the second-largest Asian group in the city of Philadelphia,[[141]](#cite_note-146) while making up the largest foreign-born population in the [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley").[[142]](#cite_note-147) A Korean community initially settled in the North Philadelphia neighborhood of [Olney](/wiki/Olney,_Philadelphia "Olney, Philadelphia"); however, the primary [Koreatown](/wiki/Koreatown,_Philadelphia "Koreatown, Philadelphia") has subsequently shifted further north, straddling the city's border with adjacent [Cheltenham](/wiki/Cheltenham,_Pennsylvania "Cheltenham, Pennsylvania") in [Montgomery County](/wiki/Montgomery_County,_Pennsylvania "Montgomery County, Pennsylvania") and [Cherry Hill](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey "Cherry Hill, New Jersey") in [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey"). South Philadelphia is home to [Vietnamese-Americans](/wiki/Vietnamese-American "Vietnamese-American") in [Little Saigon](/wiki/Little_Saigon,_Philadelphia "Little Saigon, Philadelphia") and [Cambodian-Americans](/wiki/Cambodian-Americans "Cambodian-Americans") in [Cambodia Town](/wiki/Little_Cambodia#Philadelphia,_Pennsylvania "Little Cambodia"), as well as [Thai-American](/wiki/Thai-American "Thai-American"), [Indonesian-American](/wiki/Indonesian-American "Indonesian-American"), and Chinese-American communities.

Philadelphia's [Gay village](/wiki/Gay_village "Gay village") near [Washington Square](/wiki/Washington_Square_(Philadelphia) "Washington Square (Philadelphia)") is home to a concentration of gay and lesbian-friendly businesses, restaurants, and bars.[[143]](#cite_note-148)[[144]](#cite_note-149)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Philadelphia](/wiki/Religion_in_Philadelphia "Religion in Philadelphia")

In a 2014 study by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), 68% of the population of the city identified themselves as [Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians").[[145]](#cite_note-150) Approximately 41% of Christians in the city and area professed attendance at a variety of churches that could be considered [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), while 26% professed [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") beliefs.

The [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") Christian community in Philadelphia is dominated by [mainline Protestant denominations](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") including the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America"), [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ"), the [Episcopal Church in the United States](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"), [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)") and [American Baptist Churches USA](/wiki/American_Baptist_Churches_USA "American Baptist Churches USA"). One of the most prominent mainline Protestant jurisdictions is the [Episcopal Diocese of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Episcopal_Diocese_of_Pennsylvania "Episcopal Diocese of Pennsylvania"). The [African Methodist Episcopal Church](/wiki/African_Methodist_Episcopal_Church "African Methodist Episcopal Church") was established in Philadelphia. Historically, the city has strong connections to the [Quakers](/wiki/Quakers "Quakers"), [Unitarian Universalism](/wiki/Unitarian_Universalism "Unitarian Universalism"), and the [Ethical Culture movement](/wiki/Ethical_movement "Ethical movement"), all of which continue to be represented in the city. The Quaker [Friends General Conference](/wiki/Friends_General_Conference "Friends General Conference") is based in Philadelphia. Evangelical Protestants making up less than 15% of the population were also prevalent.

Evangelical Protestant bodies included the [Anglican Church in North America](/wiki/Anglican_Church_in_North_America "Anglican Church in North America"), [Lutheran Church—Missouri Synod](/wiki/Lutheran_Church%E2%80%93Missouri_Synod "Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod"), [Presbyterian Church in America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_America "Presbyterian Church in America"), and [National Baptist Convention of America](/wiki/National_Baptist_Convention_of_America_International,_Inc. "National Baptist Convention of America International, Inc."). The Catholic community is primarily served by the [Latin](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") [Catholic Archdiocese of Philadelphia](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Philadelphia "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Philadelphia"), the [Ukrainian Catholic Archeparchy of Philadelphia](/wiki/Ukrainian_Catholic_Archeparchy_of_Philadelphia "Ukrainian Catholic Archeparchy of Philadelphia"), and the [Syro-Malankara Catholic Eparchy of the United States of America and Canada](/wiki/Syro-Malankara_Catholic_Eparchy_of_the_United_States_of_America_and_Canada "Syro-Malankara Catholic Eparchy of the United States of America and Canada"), though some [independent Catholic churches](/wiki/Independent_Catholicism "Independent Catholicism") exist throughout Philadelphia and its suburbs. The Latin Church-based jurisdiction is headquartered in the city, and its see is the [Cathedral Basilica of Saints Peter and Paul](/wiki/Cathedral_Basilica_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul_(Philadelphia) "Cathedral Basilica of Saints Peter and Paul (Philadelphia)"). The Ukrainian Catholic jurisdiction is headquartered in Philadelphia, and is seated at the [Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Cathedral_of_the_Immaculate_Conception_(Philadelphia) "Cathedral of the Immaculate Conception (Philadelphia)").

Less than 1% of Philadelphia's Christians were [Mormons](/wiki/Mormons "Mormons"). The remainder of the Christian demographic is spread among smaller Protestant denominations and the [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches") among others. The [Diocese of Eastern Pennsylvania](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_in_America_Diocese_of_Eastern_Pennsylvania "Orthodox Church in America Diocese of Eastern Pennsylvania") ([Orthodox Church in America](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_in_America "Orthodox Church in America")) and [Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Archdiocese_of_America "Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of America") ([Ecumenical Patriarchate](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople")) divide the Eastern Orthodox in Philadelphia. The [Russian Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") [St. Andrew's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Andrew%27s_Cathedral,_Philadelphia "St. Andrew's Cathedral, Philadelphia") is in the city.
The same study says that other religions collectively compose about 8% of the population, including [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), and [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism").[[146]](#cite_note-151) Philadelphia has the fifth-largest [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") population among U.S. cities.[[147]](#cite_note-152) The remaining 24% claimed [no religious affiliation](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion").

The Philadelphia [metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_Metropolitan_Statistical_Areas "List of Metropolitan Statistical Areas")'s [Jewish](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Philadelphia "History of the Jews in Philadelphia") population was estimated at 206,000 in 2001, which was the sixth-largest in the U.S. at that time.[[148]](#cite_note-153) Jewish traders were operating in southeastern Pennsylvania long before [William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn "William Penn"). Jews in Philadelphia took a prominent part in the [War of Independence](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). Although the majority of the early Jewish residents were of Portuguese or Spanish descent, some among them had emigrated from Germany and Poland. About the beginning of the 19th century, a number of Jews from the latter countries, finding the services of the [Congregation Mickvé Israel](/wiki/Congregation_Mikveh_Israel "Congregation Mikveh Israel") unfamiliar to them, resolved to form a new congregation which would use the ritual to which they had been accustomed.

[African diasporic religions](/wiki/Afro-American_religion "Afro-American religion") are practiced in some Latino and Hispanic and Caribbean communities in North and West Philadelphia.[[149]](#cite_note-154)[[150]](#cite_note-155)

### Languages

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philadelphia&action=edit), 79.12% (1,112,441) of Philadelphia residents age 5 and older spoke English at home as a [primary language](/wiki/Primary_language "Primary language"), while 9.72% (136,688) spoke Spanish, 1.64% (23,075) Chinese, 0.89% (12,499) [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"), 0.77% (10,885) Russian, 0.66% (9,240) French, 0.61% (8,639) [other Asian languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Asia "Languages of Asia"), 0.58% (8,217) [African languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Africa "Languages of Africa"), 0.56% (7,933) [Cambodian](/wiki/Cambodian_language "Cambodian language") ([Mon-Khmer](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages "Austroasiatic languages")), and Italian was spoken as a [main language](/wiki/Main_language "Main language") by 0.55% (7,773) of the population over the age of five. In total, 20.88% (293,544) of Philadelphia's population age 5 and older spoke a [mother language](/wiki/Mother_language "Mother language") other than English.[[151]](#cite_note-156)

### Poverty

Philadelphia is home to many food poverty programs, of which two of the largest are [Philabundance](/wiki/Philabundance "Philabundance") which claims to feed 90000 people per week.[[152]](#cite_note-157)[[153]](#cite_note-158)[[154]](#cite_note-159)[[155]](#cite_note-160) and [Share Food Program](/wiki/Share_Food_Program "Share Food Program") which claims to feed 1 million people per month.[[156]](#cite_note-161)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Philadelphia](/wiki/Economy_of_Philadelphia "Economy of Philadelphia")

Philadelphia's close geographical and transportation connections to other large metropolitan economies along the [Eastern Seaboard](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") of the United States have been cited as offering a significant competitive advantage for business creation and [entrepreneurship](/wiki/Entrepreneurship "Entrepreneurship").[[158]](#cite_note-163) The city is the center of economic activity in both [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") and the four-state [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") metropolitan region. Five [Fortune 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies are based in the city. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philadelphia&action=edit), the Philadelphia metropolitan area is estimated to produce a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product") (GMP) of US$479 billion,[[159]](#cite_note-164) an increase from the $445 billion calculated by the [Bureau of Economic Analysis](/wiki/Bureau_of_Economic_Analysis "Bureau of Economic Analysis") for 2017,[[160]](#cite_note-165) representing the [ninth-largest U.S. metropolitan economy](/wiki/List_of_U.S._metropolitan_areas_by_GDP "List of U.S. metropolitan areas by GDP").

Philadelphia's economic sectors include [financial services](/wiki/Financial_services "Financial services"), health care, [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), information technology, trade and transportation, manufacturing, [oil refining](/wiki/Oil_refinery "Oil refinery"), [food processing](/wiki/Food_processing "Food processing"), and tourism. Metropolitan Philadelphia is one of the top five American [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") hubs, credited to its proximity to New York City's [financial](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan") and [tech and biotechnology ecosystems](/wiki/List_of_tech_companies_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "List of tech companies in the New York metropolitan area").[[31]](#cite_note-Maloney-2024-31) Financial activities account for the largest economic sector of the metropolitan area, which is one of the largest [health education](/wiki/Health_education "Health education") and research centers in the United States. The city's two largest employers are the federal and city governments. Philadelphia's largest private employer is the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") followed by [Children's Hospital of Philadelphia](/wiki/Children%27s_Hospital_of_Philadelphia "Children's Hospital of Philadelphia").[[161]](#cite_note-pewtrusts.org-2015-166)

### Finance and corporations

Main article: [List of companies based in the Philadelphia area](/wiki/List_of_companies_based_in_the_Philadelphia_area "List of companies based in the Philadelphia area")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/2022_Old_Philadelphia_Stock_Exchange_Buiding%2C_1411_Walnut_Street%2C_close.jpg/250px-2022_Old_Philadelphia_Stock_Exchange_Buiding%2C_1411_Walnut_Street%2C_close.jpg)](/wiki/File:2022_Old_Philadelphia_Stock_Exchange_Buiding,_1411_Walnut_Street,_close.jpg)

The [Philadelphia Stock Exchange](/wiki/Philadelphia_Stock_Exchange "Philadelphia Stock Exchange") building, the nation's first [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange"), at 1411 [Walnut Street](/wiki/Walnut_Street_(Philadelphia) "Walnut Street (Philadelphia)")

The [Philadelphia Stock Exchange](/wiki/Philadelphia_Stock_Exchange "Philadelphia Stock Exchange"), acquired by [Nasdaq](/wiki/Nasdaq "Nasdaq") in 2007, is a global leader in [options](/wiki/Option_(finance) "Option (finance)") trading.[[33]](#cite_note-Lucas_Downey_and_Somer_Anderson-2022-33) The city is home to the headquarters of [Comcast](/wiki/Comcast "Comcast"), the nation's largest [multinational](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation") telecommunications corporation; insurance conglomerates [Cigna](/wiki/Cigna "Cigna"), [Colonial Penn](/wiki/Colonial_Penn "Colonial Penn"), and [Independence Blue Cross](/wiki/Independence_Blue_Cross "Independence Blue Cross"); as well as food services company [Aramark](/wiki/Aramark "Aramark"), chemical makers [FMC Corporation](/wiki/FMC_Corporation "FMC Corporation") and [Rohm and Haas](/wiki/Rohm_and_Haas "Rohm and Haas"), pharmaceutical companies [GlaxoSmithKline](/wiki/GlaxoSmithKline "GlaxoSmithKline"), [Amicus Therapeutics](/wiki/Amicus_Therapeutics "Amicus Therapeutics"), [Spark Therapeutics](/wiki/Spark_Therapeutics "Spark Therapeutics"), [apparel](/wiki/Apparel "Apparel") retailers [Five Below](/wiki/Five_Below "Five Below") and [Urban Outfitters](/wiki/Urban_Outfitters "Urban Outfitters") and its subsidiary [Anthropologie](/wiki/Anthropologie "Anthropologie"), automotive parts retailer [Pep Boys](/wiki/Pep_Boys "Pep Boys"), and stainless steel producer [Carpenter Technology Corporation](/wiki/Carpenter_Technology_Corporation "Carpenter Technology Corporation").

Other corporate headquarters in the city include [Crown Holdings](/wiki/Crown_Holdings "Crown Holdings"), and [Brandywine Realty Trust](/wiki/Brandywine_Realty_Trust "Brandywine Realty Trust"). The headquarters of [Boeing Rotorcraft Systems](/wiki/Boeing_Rotorcraft_Systems "Boeing Rotorcraft Systems") and its main [rotorcraft](/wiki/Rotorcraft "Rotorcraft") factory are in the Philadelphia suburb of [Ridley Park](/wiki/Ridley_Park,_Pennsylvania "Ridley Park, Pennsylvania"); [The Vanguard Group](/wiki/The_Vanguard_Group "The Vanguard Group") and the U.S. headquarters of [Siemens Healthineers](/wiki/Siemens_Healthineers "Siemens Healthineers") are headquartered in [Malvern, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Malvern,_Pennsylvania "Malvern, Pennsylvania"), a Philadelphia suburb. Healthcare [conglomerate](/wiki/Conglomerate_(company) "Conglomerate (company)") [AmerisourceBergen](/wiki/AmerisourceBergen "AmerisourceBergen") and the North American headquarters of furniture retailer [IKEA](/wiki/IKEA "IKEA") are located in suburban [Conshohocken, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Conshohocken,_Pennsylvania "Conshohocken, Pennsylvania"). Across the [Delaware River](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") in adjacent [Camden County, New Jersey](/wiki/Camden_County,_New_Jersey "Camden County, New Jersey"), [Campbell Soup Company](/wiki/Campbell_Soup_Company "Campbell Soup Company") and [Subaru USA](/wiki/Subaru "Subaru") are both headquartered in [Camden, New Jersey](/wiki/Camden,_New_Jersey "Camden, New Jersey"), and [TD Bank (USA)](/wiki/TD_Bank_(USA) "TD Bank (USA)") is headquartered in [nearby](/wiki/Inner_suburb "Inner suburb") suburban [Cherry Hill, New Jersey](/wiki/Cherry_Hill,_New_Jersey "Cherry Hill, New Jersey").

### Tech and biotech

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/View_of_Center_City_%28Comcast_Technology_Center%29.jpg/250px-View_of_Center_City_%28Comcast_Technology_Center%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_of_Center_City_(Comcast_Technology_Center).jpg)

[Comcast Technology Center](/wiki/Comcast_Technology_Center "Comcast Technology Center") in Center City is the tallest building in Philadelphia.

Philadelphia is a hub for information technology and [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology").[[162]](#cite_note-167) Philadelphia and Pennsylvania are attracting new [life sciences](/wiki/Life_sciences "Life sciences") ventures.[[163]](#cite_note-Archived_June_10-168) As of 2024, the [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") ranks as one of the Big Five U.S. [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") hubs, enabled by its proximity to both the [entrepreneurial](/wiki/Tech_companies_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Tech companies in the New York metropolitan area") and [financial ecosystems](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") of New York City and to the [federal regulatory environment](/wiki/U.S._government "U.S. government") of Washington, D.C.[[31]](#cite_note-Maloney-2024-31)[[163]](#cite_note-Archived_June_10-168)

### Tourism

See also: [List of tourist attractions in Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_tourist_attractions_in_Philadelphia "List of tourist attractions in Philadelphia")

Philadelphia's history attracts many tourists, with the [Independence National Historical Park](/wiki/Independence_National_Historical_Park "Independence National Historical Park"), which includes the [Liberty Bell](/wiki/Liberty_Bell "Liberty Bell"), [Independence Hall](/wiki/Independence_Hall "Independence Hall"), and other historic sites, received over 5 million visitors in 2016.[[164]](#cite_note-169) The city welcomed 42 million domestic tourists in 2016 who spent $6.8 billion, generating an estimated $11 billion in total economic impact in the city and surrounding four counties of Pennsylvania.[[165]](#cite_note-170) The annual [Naked Bike Ride](/wiki/World_Naked_Bike_Ride "World Naked Bike Ride") attracts participants from around the United States and internationally to Philadelphia.

### Trade and transportation

Main article: [Transportation in Philadelphia](/wiki/Transportation_in_Philadelphia "Transportation in Philadelphia")

[Philadelphia International Airport](/wiki/Philadelphia_International_Airport "Philadelphia International Airport"), a major [Transatlantic](/wiki/Transatlantic_flight "Transatlantic flight") gateway and transcontinental hub, has undergone a $900 million [infrastructural](/wiki/Infrastructure "Infrastructure") expansion to increase passenger capacity and augment passenger experience, and the airport continues an ongoing capital expenditure program to upgrade facilities and add further amenities.[[166]](#cite_note-171)[[167]](#cite_note-172) The [Port of Philadelphia](/wiki/Port_of_Philadelphia "Port of Philadelphia"), having experienced the highest percentage growth by [tonnage](/wiki/Twenty-foot_equivalent_unit "Twenty-foot equivalent unit") loaded in 2017 among major U.S. seaports, has doubled its shipping [capacity](/wiki/Tonnage "Tonnage") to accommodate super-sized [post-Panamax](/wiki/Post-Panamax "Post-Panamax") shipping vessels since 2018.[[168]](#cite_note-173) Philadelphia's [30th Street Station](/wiki/30th_Street_Station "30th Street Station") is the third-busiest [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") rail hub, following [Penn Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(New_York) "Pennsylvania Station (New York)") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and [Union Station](/wiki/Washington_Union_Station "Washington Union Station") in Washington, D.C., transporting over 4 million [inter-city rail](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") passengers annually.[[169]](#cite_note-www.amtrak.com-174)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Philadelphia](/wiki/Culture_of_Philadelphia "Culture of Philadelphia")

See also: [List of National Historic Landmarks in Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Philadelphia "List of National Historic Landmarks in Philadelphia"), [List of sites of interest in Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_sites_of_interest_in_Philadelphia "List of sites of interest in Philadelphia"), and [List of tourist attractions in Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_tourist_attractions_in_Philadelphia "List of tourist attractions in Philadelphia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Kimmel_Center_cropped.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Kimmel_Center_cropped.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kimmel_Center_cropped.tif)

The [Kimmel Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Kimmel_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Kimmel Center for the Performing Arts") at 300 [Broad Street](/wiki/Broad_Street_(Philadelphia) "Broad Street (Philadelphia)"), home of the [Philadelphia Orchestra](/wiki/Philadelphia_Orchestra "Philadelphia Orchestra")

Philadelphia is home to many [national historical sites](/wiki/National_Historic_Site_(United_States) "National Historic Site (United States)") that relate to the founding of the United States. [Independence National Historical Park](/wiki/Independence_National_Historical_Park "Independence National Historical Park") is the center of these historical landmarks and one of the country's 22 [UNESCO World Heritage Sites](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site"). [Independence Hall](/wiki/Independence_Hall "Independence Hall"), where the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") was signed, and the [Liberty Bell](/wiki/Liberty_Bell "Liberty Bell") is housed, are among the city's most popular attractions. Other national historic sites include the homes of [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe_National_Historic_Site "Edgar Allan Poe National Historic Site") and [Thaddeus Kosciuszko](/wiki/Thaddeus_Kosciuszko_National_Memorial "Thaddeus Kosciuszko National Memorial"), and early government buildings, including the [First](/wiki/First_Bank_of_the_United_States "First Bank of the United States") and the [Second Bank of the United States](/wiki/Second_Bank_of_the_United_States "Second Bank of the United States"), [Fort Mifflin](/wiki/Fort_Mifflin "Fort Mifflin"), and the [Gloria Dei (Old Swedes') Church](/wiki/Gloria_Dei_(Old_Swedes%27)_Church "Gloria Dei (Old Swedes') Church").[[170]](#cite_note-National_Park_Service-2017-175) Philadelphia alone has 67 [National Historic Landmarks](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"), the third most of any city in the country.[[170]](#cite_note-National_Park_Service-2017-175)

Philadelphia's major science museums include the [Franklin Institute](/wiki/Franklin_Institute "Franklin Institute"), which contains the [Benjamin Franklin National Memorial](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_National_Memorial "Benjamin Franklin National Memorial"), the [Academy of Natural Sciences](/wiki/Academy_of_Natural_Sciences_of_Drexel_University "Academy of Natural Sciences of Drexel University"), the [Mütter Museum](/wiki/M%C3%BCtter_Museum "Mütter Museum"), and the [University of Pennsylvania Museum of Archaeology and Anthropology](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania_Museum_of_Archaeology_and_Anthropology "University of Pennsylvania Museum of Archaeology and Anthropology"). History museums include the [National Constitution Center](/wiki/National_Constitution_Center "National Constitution Center"), the [Museum of the American Revolution](/wiki/Museum_of_the_American_Revolution "Museum of the American Revolution"), the [Philadelphia History Museum](/wiki/Philadelphia_History_Museum "Philadelphia History Museum"), the [National Museum of American Jewish History](/wiki/National_Museum_of_American_Jewish_History "National Museum of American Jewish History"), the [African American Museum in Philadelphia](/wiki/African_American_Museum_in_Philadelphia "African American Museum in Philadelphia"), the [Historical Society of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Historical_Society_of_Pennsylvania "Historical Society of Pennsylvania"), the Masonic Library and Museum of Pennsylvania in the [Masonic Temple](/wiki/Masonic_Temple_(Philadelphia,_Pennsylvania) "Masonic Temple (Philadelphia, Pennsylvania)"), and the [Eastern State Penitentiary](/wiki/Eastern_State_Penitentiary "Eastern State Penitentiary"). Philadelphia is home to the United States's first [zoo](/wiki/Philadelphia_Zoo "Philadelphia Zoo")[[171]](#cite_note-176) and [hospital](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Hospital "Pennsylvania Hospital"),[[172]](#cite_note-177) as well as [Fairmount Park](/wiki/Fairmount_Park "Fairmount Park"), one of America's oldest and largest urban parks,[[22]](#cite_note-tpl.org-22) founded in 1855.[[173]](#cite_note-178)

The city is home to important archival repositories, including the [Library Company of Philadelphia](/wiki/Library_Company_of_Philadelphia "Library Company of Philadelphia"), established in 1731 by [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin") at 1314 [Locust Street](/wiki/Locust_Street "Locust Street"),[[174]](#cite_note-179) and the [Athenaeum of Philadelphia](/wiki/Athenaeum_of_Philadelphia "Athenaeum of Philadelphia"), founded in 1814.[[175]](#cite_note-180) The [Presbyterian Historical Society](/wiki/Presbyterian_Historical_Society "Presbyterian Historical Society") is the country's oldest denominational historical society, organized in 1852.[[176]](#cite_note-181)

### Arts

See also: [List of museums in Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Philadelphia "List of museums in Philadelphia"), [List of public art in Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_public_art_in_Philadelphia "List of public art in Philadelphia"), and [Mummers Parade](/wiki/Mummers_Parade "Mummers Parade")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art%2C_main_building.jpg/250px-Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art%2C_main_building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art,_main_building.jpg)

The [Philadelphia Museum of Art](/wiki/Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art "Philadelphia Museum of Art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Keys_To_Community_crop.jpg/250px-Keys_To_Community_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Keys_To_Community_crop.jpg)

*Keys to Community*, a bust of [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin") by [James Peniston](/wiki/James_Peniston "James Peniston") at 325 [Arch Street](/wiki/Arch_Street_(Philadelphia) "Arch Street (Philadelphia)") in [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia")

The city is home to multiple art museums, including the [Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Academy_of_the_Fine_Arts "Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts") and the [Rodin Museum](/wiki/Rodin_Museum "Rodin Museum"), which holds the largest collection of work by [Auguste Rodin](/wiki/Auguste_Rodin "Auguste Rodin") outside France. The city's largest art museum, the [Philadelphia Museum of Art](/wiki/Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art "Philadelphia Museum of Art"), is one of the [largest art museums in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_art_museums "List of largest art museums"). The long flight of [steps](/wiki/Rocky_Steps "Rocky Steps") to the Art Museum's main entrance became famous after the film *[Rocky](/wiki/Rocky "Rocky")* (1976).[[177]](#cite_note-Weeks-2006-182)

Annual events include the [Philadelphia Film Festival](/wiki/Philadelphia_Film_Festival "Philadelphia Film Festival"), held annually each October, the [6abc Dunkin' Donuts Thanksgiving Day Parade](/wiki/6abc_Dunkin%27_Donuts_Thanksgiving_Day_Parade "6abc Dunkin' Donuts Thanksgiving Day Parade"), the nation's longest-running continuously held [Thanksgiving Day](/wiki/Thanksgiving_Day "Thanksgiving Day") parade, and the [Mummers Parade](/wiki/Mummers_Parade "Mummers Parade"), the nation's longest continuously held folk parade, which is held every New Year's Day predominantly on [Broad Street](/wiki/Broad_Street_(Philadelphia) "Broad Street (Philadelphia)").

Areas such as [South Street](/wiki/South_Street_(Philadelphia) "South Street (Philadelphia)") and the [Old City](/wiki/Old_City_(Philadelphia) "Old City (Philadelphia)") section of the city have a vibrant night life. The [Avenue of the Arts](/wiki/Avenue_of_the_Arts_(Philadelphia) "Avenue of the Arts (Philadelphia)") in [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia") contains many restaurants and theaters, such as the [Kimmel Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Kimmel_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Kimmel Center for the Performing Arts"), home of the [Philadelphia Orchestra](/wiki/Philadelphia_Orchestra "Philadelphia Orchestra"), and the [Academy of Music](/wiki/Academy_of_Music_(Philadelphia) "Academy of Music (Philadelphia)"), home of [Opera Philadelphia](/wiki/Opera_Philadelphia "Opera Philadelphia") and the [Pennsylvania Ballet](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Ballet "Pennsylvania Ballet").[[177]](#cite_note-Weeks-2006-182) The [Wilma Theatre](/wiki/Wilma_Theater_(Philadelphia) "Wilma Theater (Philadelphia)") and the [Philadelphia Theatre Company](/wiki/Philadelphia_Theatre_Company "Philadelphia Theatre Company") at the [Suzanne Roberts Theatre](/wiki/Suzanne_Roberts_Theatre "Suzanne Roberts Theatre") produce a variety of new plays.[[178]](#cite_note-183)[[179]](#cite_note-184) Several blocks to the east are the [Lantern Theater Company](/wiki/Lantern_Theater_Company "Lantern Theater Company") at [St. Stephens Episcopal Church](/wiki/St._Stephen%27s_Episcopal_Church_(Philadelphia) "St. Stephen's Episcopal Church (Philadelphia)");[[180]](#cite_note-185) and the [Walnut Street Theatre](/wiki/Walnut_Street_Theatre "Walnut Street Theatre"), a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") stated to be the oldest and most subscribed-to [theatre](/wiki/Theater_(structure) "Theater (structure)") in the [English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world"), founded in 1809.[[181]](#cite_note-186) In May 2019, the Walnut Street Theatre announced a major expansion to begin in 2020.[[182]](#cite_note-187) [New Freedom Theatre](/wiki/New_Freedom_Theatre "New Freedom Theatre"), Pennsylvania's oldest African-American theatre, is located on North Broad Street.

Philadelphia has more [public art](/wiki/Public_art "Public art") than any other American city.[[183]](#cite_note-188) In 1872, the [Association for Public Art](/wiki/Association_for_Public_Art "Association for Public Art"), formerly the Fairmount Park Art Association, was created as the first private association in the United States dedicated to integrating public art and [urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning").[[184]](#cite_note-189) In 1959, lobbying by the Artists Equity Association helped create the [Percent for Art](/wiki/Percent_for_Art "Percent for Art") [ordinance](/wiki/Law "Law"), the first for a U.S. city.[[185]](#cite_note-190) The program, which has funded more than 200 pieces of public art, is administered by Creative Philadelphia,[[f]](#cite_note-192) the city's office of arts and culture.[[187]](#cite_note-193) The city has more murals than any other American city, due to the 1984 creation of the Department of Recreation's [Mural Arts Program](/wiki/Mural_Arts_Program "Mural Arts Program"), which seeks to beautify neighborhoods and provide an outlet for [graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti") artists. The program has funded more than 2,800 [murals](/wiki/Mural "Mural") by professional, staff and volunteer artists and educated more than 20,000 youth in underserved neighborhoods throughout Philadelphia.[[188]](#cite_note-194)

The city is home to a number of art organizations, including the regional art advocacy nonprofit Philadelphia Tri-State Artists Equity,[[189]](#cite_note-195) the [Philadelphia Sketch Club](/wiki/Philadelphia_Sketch_Club "Philadelphia Sketch Club"), one of the country's oldest artists' clubs,[[190]](#cite_note-196) and [The Plastic Club](/wiki/The_Plastic_Club "The Plastic Club"), started by women excluded from the Sketch Club.[[191]](#cite_note-197) Many [Old City](/wiki/Old_City,_Philadelphia "Old City, Philadelphia") art galleries stay open late on the [First Friday](/wiki/First_Friday_(public_event) "First Friday (public event)") event of each month.[[192]](#cite_note-198)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Cuisine of Philadelphia](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Philadelphia "Cuisine of Philadelphia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Pats_and_Genos.jpg/250px-Pats_and_Genos.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pats_and_Genos.jpg)

[Pat's Steaks](/wiki/Pat%27s_King_of_Steaks "Pat's King of Steaks") (foreground) and [Geno's Steaks](/wiki/Geno%27s_Steaks "Geno's Steaks") (background) in [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia")

The city is known for its [hoagies](/wiki/Submarine_sandwich "Submarine sandwich"), [stromboli](/wiki/Stromboli_(food) "Stromboli (food)"), [roast pork sandwich](/wiki/Roast_pork_sandwich "Roast pork sandwich"), [scrapple](/wiki/Scrapple "Scrapple"), [soft pretzels](/wiki/Pretzel#Pennsylvania "Pretzel"), [water ice](/wiki/Italian_ice "Italian ice"), [Irish potato candy](/wiki/Irish_potato_candy "Irish potato candy"), [tastykakes](/wiki/Tastykake "Tastykake"), and the [cheesesteak](/wiki/Cheesesteak "Cheesesteak") sandwich, which was developed by Italian immigrants.[[193]](#cite_note-199) The Philadelphia area has many establishments that serve cheesesteaks, including restaurants, [taverns](/wiki/Tavern "Tavern"), [delicatessens](/wiki/Delicatessen "Delicatessen") and pizza parlors.[[194]](#cite_note-200)[[195]](#cite_note-201)[[196]](#cite_note-202) The originator of the thinly sliced steak sandwich in the 1930s, initially without cheese, is [Pat's King of Steaks](/wiki/Pat%27s_King_of_Steaks "Pat's King of Steaks"), which faces its rival [Geno's Steaks](/wiki/Geno%27s_Steaks "Geno's Steaks"), founded in 1966,[[197]](#cite_note-203) across the intersection of 9th Street and Passyunk Avenue in the [Italian Market](/wiki/Italian_Market,_Philadelphia "Italian Market, Philadelphia") of [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia").[[198]](#cite_note-204)

[McGillin's Olde Ale House](/wiki/McGillin%27s_Olde_Ale_House "McGillin's Olde Ale House"), opened in 1860 on Drury Street in [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia"), is the oldest continuously operated tavern in the city.[[199]](#cite_note-205) The [City Tavern](/wiki/City_Tavern "City Tavern") is a replica of a historic 18th-century building first opened in 1773, demolished in 1854 after a fire, and rebuilt in 1975 on the same site as part of [Independence National Historical Park](/wiki/Independence_National_Historical_Park "Independence National Historical Park").[[200]](#cite_note-206) The tavern offers authentic 18th-century recipes, served in seven period dining rooms, three wine cellar rooms and an outdoor garden.[[201]](#cite_note-207)

The [Reading Terminal Market](/wiki/Reading_Terminal_Market "Reading Terminal Market") is a historic [food market](/wiki/Marketplace "Marketplace") founded in 1893 in the [Reading Terminal](/wiki/Reading_Terminal "Reading Terminal") building, a designated National Historic Landmark. The enclosed market is one of the oldest and largest markets in the country, hosting over a hundred merchants offering [Pennsylvania Dutch](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Dutch "Pennsylvania Dutch") specialties, [artisan cheese](/wiki/Artisan_cheese "Artisan cheese") and meat, locally grown groceries, and specialty and ethnic foods.[[202]](#cite_note-208)

### Dialect

Main article: [Philadelphia English](/wiki/Philadelphia_English "Philadelphia English")

The traditional Philadelphia accent is considered by some [linguists](/wiki/Linguist "Linguist") to be the most distinctive accent in North America.[[203]](#cite_note-209) The Philadelphia dialect, which is spread throughout the [Delaware Valley](/wiki/Delaware_Valley "Delaware Valley") and [South Jersey](/wiki/South_Jersey "South Jersey"), is part of a larger [Mid-Atlantic American English](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_American_English "Mid-Atlantic American English") family, a designation that also includes the [Baltimore accent](/wiki/Baltimore_accent "Baltimore accent"). Additionally, it shares many similarities with the [New York accent](/wiki/New_York_accent "New York accent"). Owing to over a century of linguistic data collected by researchers at the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") under sociolinguist [William Labov](/wiki/William_Labov "William Labov"), the Philadelphia dialect has been one of the best-studied forms of [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English").[[204]](#cite_note-210)[[205]](#cite_note-211)[[g]](#cite_note-212) The accent is especially found within the Irish American and Italian American working-class neighborhoods.[[206]](#cite_note-213) Philadelphia also has its own unique collection of [neologisms](/wiki/Neologism "Neologism") and slang terms.[[207]](#cite_note-214)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Philadelphia](/wiki/Music_of_Philadelphia "Music of Philadelphia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Curtisinstofmusic.JPG/250px-Curtisinstofmusic.JPG)](/wiki/File:Curtisinstofmusic.JPG)

The [Curtis Institute of Music](/wiki/Curtis_Institute_of_Music "Curtis Institute of Music") at 1726 [Locust Street](/wiki/Locust_Street "Locust Street") in [Center City](/wiki/Center_City,_Philadelphia "Center City, Philadelphia"), one of the world's premier [conservatories](/wiki/Music_school "Music school")

The [Philadelphia Orchestra](/wiki/Philadelphia_Orchestra "Philadelphia Orchestra") is generally considered one of the [top five orchestras](/wiki/Big_Five_(orchestras) "Big Five (orchestras)") in the United States. The orchestra performs at the [Kimmel Center](/wiki/Kimmel_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Kimmel Center for the Performing Arts")[[208]](#cite_note-215) and has a [summer concert series](/wiki/Concert#Venues "Concert") at the [Mann Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Mann_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Mann Center for the Performing Arts").[[209]](#cite_note-216) [Opera Philadelphia](/wiki/Opera_Philadelphia "Opera Philadelphia") performs at the nation's oldest continually operating opera house—the [Academy of Music](/wiki/Academy_of_Music_(Philadelphia) "Academy of Music (Philadelphia)").[[177]](#cite_note-Weeks-2006-182) The [Philadelphia Boys Choir & Chorale](/wiki/Philadelphia_Boys_Choir_%26_Chorale "Philadelphia Boys Choir & Chorale") has performed its music all over the world.[[210]](#cite_note-217) The [Philly Pops](/wiki/Philly_Pops "Philly Pops") plays orchestral versions of popular [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [swing](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music"), [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre"), and [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues") songs at the Kimmel Center and other venues within the [mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_(United_States) "Mid-Atlantic (United States)") region.[[211]](#cite_note-218) The [Curtis Institute of Music](/wiki/Curtis_Institute_of_Music "Curtis Institute of Music") is one of the world's premier [conservatories](/wiki/Music_school "Music school") and among the most selective institutes of higher education in the nation.[[212]](#cite_note-219)

Philadelphia has played a prominent role in the [music of the United States](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_States "Music of the United States"). The culture of [American popular music](/wiki/American_popular_music "American popular music") has been influenced by significant contributions of Philadelphia area musicians and producers, in both the recording and broadcasting industries. In 1952, the teen dance party program called *Bandstand* premiered on local television, hosted by [Bob Horn](/wiki/Bob_Horn_(broadcaster) "Bob Horn (broadcaster)"). The show was renamed *[American Bandstand](/wiki/American_Bandstand "American Bandstand")* in 1957, when it began national syndication on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company"), hosted by [Dick Clark](/wiki/Dick_Clark "Dick Clark") and produced in Philadelphia until 1964 when it moved to Los Angeles.[[213]](#cite_note-220) Promoters marketed youthful musical artists known as [teen idols](/wiki/Teen_idol "Teen idol") to appeal to the young audience. Philadelphia-born singers, including [Frankie Avalon](/wiki/Frankie_Avalon "Frankie Avalon"), [James Darren](/wiki/James_Darren "James Darren"), [Eddie Fisher](/wiki/Eddie_Fisher "Eddie Fisher"), [Fabian Forte](/wiki/Fabian_Forte "Fabian Forte"), [Bobby Rydell](/wiki/Bobby_Rydell "Bobby Rydell"), and [South Philly](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia")-raised [Chubby Checker](/wiki/Chubby_Checker "Chubby Checker"), topped the music charts, establishing a clean-cut [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll") image.

[Philly soul](/wiki/Philadelphia_soul "Philadelphia soul") music of the late 1960s–1970s is a highly produced version of [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") which led to later forms of popular music such as [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") and [urban contemporary](/wiki/Urban_contemporary "Urban contemporary") [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues").[[214]](#cite_note-221) On July 13, 1985, [John F. Kennedy Stadium](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_Stadium_(Philadelphia) "John F. Kennedy Stadium (Philadelphia)") was the American venue for the [Live Aid](/wiki/Live_Aid "Live Aid") concert.[[215]](#cite_note-222) The city also hosted the [Live 8](/wiki/Live_8 "Live 8") concert, which attracted about 700,000 people to the [Benjamin Franklin Parkway](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin_Parkway "Benjamin Franklin Parkway") on July 2, 2005.[[216]](#cite_note-223)

Notable rock and pop musicians from Philadelphia and its suburbs include [Bill Haley & His Comets](/wiki/Bill_Haley_%26_His_Comets "Bill Haley & His Comets"), [Nazz](/wiki/Nazz "Nazz"), [Todd Rundgren](/wiki/Todd_Rundgren "Todd Rundgren"), [Hall & Oates](/wiki/Hall_%26_Oates "Hall & Oates"), [the Hooters](/wiki/The_Hooters "The Hooters"), [Cinderella](/wiki/Cinderella_(band) "Cinderella (band)"), [DJ Jazzy Jeff & the Fresh Prince](/wiki/DJ_Jazzy_Jeff_%26_the_Fresh_Prince "DJ Jazzy Jeff & the Fresh Prince"), [Ween](/wiki/Ween "Ween"), [Schoolly D](/wiki/Schoolly_D "Schoolly D"), [Pink](/wiki/Pink_(singer) "Pink (singer)"), [the Roots](/wiki/The_Roots "The Roots"), [Beanie Sigel](/wiki/Beanie_Sigel "Beanie Sigel"), [State Property](/wiki/State_Property_(band) "State Property (band)"), [Lisa "Left Eye" Lopes](/wiki/Lisa_Lopes "Lisa Lopes"), [Meek Mill](/wiki/Meek_Mill "Meek Mill"), [Lil Uzi Vert](/wiki/Lil_Uzi_Vert "Lil Uzi Vert"), and others.

## Sports

Main article: [Sports in Philadelphia](/wiki/Sports_in_Philadelphia "Sports in Philadelphia")

See also: [Philadelphia 76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers"), [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers"), [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles"), [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies"), and [Philadelphia Union](/wiki/Philadelphia_Union "Philadelphia Union")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Citizens_Bank_Park_2021.jpg/250px-Citizens_Bank_Park_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Citizens_Bank_Park_2021.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Lincoln_Financial_Field%2C_Philadelphia.jpg/250px-Lincoln_Financial_Field%2C_Philadelphia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincoln_Financial_Field,_Philadelphia.jpg)

[Citizens Bank Park](/wiki/Citizens_Bank_Park "Citizens Bank Park") (left), home of the [Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") since 2004, and [Lincoln Financial Field](/wiki/Lincoln_Financial_Field "Lincoln Financial Field") (right), home of the [Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") since 2003

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Super_Bowl_402EF3AA.jpg/250px-Super_Bowl_402EF3AA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Super_Bowl_402EF3AA.jpg)

The [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") are awarded the [Vince Lombardi Trophy](/wiki/Vince_Lombardi_Trophy "Vince Lombardi Trophy") after winning [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII"), on February 4, 2018.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/WellsFargoCenterPhila_29.JPG/250px-WellsFargoCenterPhila_29.JPG)](/wiki/File:WellsFargoCenterPhila_29.JPG)

[Xfinity Mobile Arena](/wiki/Xfinity_Mobile_Arena "Xfinity Mobile Arena") in [South Philadelphia](/wiki/South_Philadelphia "South Philadelphia"), home of the two-time [Stanley Cup](/wiki/Stanley_Cup "Stanley Cup") champion [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) and three-time champion [Philadelphia 76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/A358%2C_Philadelphia%2C_Pennsylvania%2C_USA%2C_Boathouse_Row_at_night%2C_2009.JPG/250px-A358%2C_Philadelphia%2C_Pennsylvania%2C_USA%2C_Boathouse_Row_at_night%2C_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:A358,_Philadelphia,_Pennsylvania,_USA,_Boathouse_Row_at_night,_2009.JPG)

Historic [Boathouse Row](/wiki/Boathouse_Row "Boathouse Row") at night on the [Schuylkill River](/wiki/Schuylkill_River "Schuylkill River"), a symbol of the city's rich history in competitive [rowing](/wiki/Rowing "Rowing")

Philadelphia has one of the nation's richest histories in professional sports, dating back to the mid-19th century. Its first professional sports team, the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics_(1860%E2%80%931876) "Philadelphia Athletics (1860–1876)"), a professional baseball team, was founded in 1860.[[217]](#cite_note-224) The Athletics were initially an [amateur league](/wiki/National_Association_of_Base_Ball_Players "National Association of Base Ball Players") team that [turned professional](/wiki/National_Association_of_Professional_Base_Ball_Players "National Association of Professional Base Ball Players") in 1871. In 1876, the Athletics joined with seven other teams in founding the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)"), now the longest continuously operating league in world sports.[[218]](#cite_note-225)

Philadelphia is one of 12 U.S. cities to have teams in [all four major league sports](/wiki/United_States_cities_with_teams_from_four_major_league_sports "United States cities with teams from four major league sports"): the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB), the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL), and the [Philadelphia 76ers](/wiki/Philadelphia_76ers "Philadelphia 76ers") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA).[[219]](#cite_note-226) The Phillies, formed in 1883 as the Quakers and renamed in 1884,[[220]](#cite_note-227) are the oldest team continuously playing under the same name in the same city in the history of American professional sports.[[221]](#cite_note-228)

The Philadelphia metro area is also home to the [Philadelphia Union](/wiki/Philadelphia_Union "Philadelphia Union") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") (MLS), plays their home games at [Subaru Park](/wiki/Subaru_Park "Subaru Park"), a [soccer-specific stadium](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium") in [Chester, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Chester,_Pennsylvania "Chester, Pennsylvania").[[222]](#cite_note-229)[[223]](#cite_note-230)[[224]](#cite_note-231)

Philadelphia was the second of eight U.S. cities to win titles in all four major leagues, the MLB, NFL, NHL, and NBA. It won a title in soccer in the now-defunct [North American Soccer League](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League "North American Soccer League") in 1973. Following the 76ers' victory over the [Los Angeles Lakers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Lakers "Los Angeles Lakers") in the [1983 NBA Finals](/wiki/1983_NBA_Finals "1983 NBA Finals"), however, the city's professional teams and their fans endured 25 years without a championship in any professional sport[[225]](#cite_note-232) until the Phillies won the [2008 World Series](/wiki/2008_World_Series "2008 World Series"), defeating the [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Rays "Tampa Bay Rays").[[226]](#cite_note-233)[[227]](#cite_note-234) This quarter century without a championship for any Philadelphia sports team is sometimes described as the [Curse of Billy Penn](/wiki/Curse_of_Billy_Penn "Curse of Billy Penn"), a reference to a 1987 decision that permitted [One Liberty Place](/wiki/Liberty_Place "Liberty Place") to become the first building in city history to surpass the height of *[William Penn](/wiki/William_Penn_(Calder) "William Penn (Calder)")*, a statue installed in 1894 atop [City Hall](/wiki/Philadelphia_City_Hall "Philadelphia City Hall").[[228]](#cite_note-235) In 2004, during the city's championship drought, [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") placed Philadelphia second on its list of "The Fifteen Most Tortured Sports Cities".[[229]](#cite_note-236)[[230]](#cite_note-237) The city's sports fans are often both praised and sometimes derided. In 2011, for instance, *[GQ](/wiki/GQ "GQ")* magazine named Eagles and Phillies fans the nation's worst professional sports fans, describing them as the "Meanest Fans in America" in summarizing repeated incidents of their drunken behavior and long history of [booing](/wiki/Booing "Booing").[[231]](#cite_note-238)[[232]](#cite_note-239)

After the Phillies won the 2008 World Series, nine years passed without a championship until the Eagles won their first [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII") following the [2017 season](/wiki/2017_NFL_season "2017 NFL season"), defeating the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") in [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII"). Seven seasons later, following the [2024 season](/wiki/2024_NFL_season "2024 NFL season"), the Eagles won their second Super Bowl, defeating the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") in [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX").[[233]](#cite_note-240)

Major professional sports teams that originated in Philadelphia, which later moved to other cities, include the [Golden State Warriors](/wiki/Golden_State_Warriors "Golden State Warriors") basketball team, which played in Philadelphia from 1946 to 1962[[234]](#cite_note-241) and the [Oakland Athletics](/wiki/Oakland_Athletics "Oakland Athletics") baseball team, which was originally the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/History_of_the_Philadelphia_Athletics "History of the Philadelphia Athletics") and played in Philadelphia from 1901 to 1954.[[235]](#cite_note-242)

Philadelphia is home to professional, semi-professional, and elite amateur teams in multiple other sports, including [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league"), and [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"). Major running events in the city include the [Penn Relays](/wiki/Penn_Relays "Penn Relays"), the [Philadelphia Marathon](/wiki/Philadelphia_Marathon "Philadelphia Marathon"), and the [Broad Street Run](/wiki/Broad_Street_Run "Broad Street Run"). The [Collegiate Rugby Championship](/wiki/Collegiate_Rugby_Championship "Collegiate Rugby Championship") is played annually each June at [Talen Energy Stadium](/wiki/Talen_Energy_Stadium "Talen Energy Stadium") in Chester.[[236]](#cite_note-243)

The city also has a rich history in [rowing](/wiki/Rowing_(sport) "Rowing (sport)"), which has been popular in Philadelphia since the 18th century.[[237]](#cite_note-244) On [Boathouse Row](/wiki/Boathouse_Row "Boathouse Row"), a symbol of Philadelphia's rich rowing history, each [Big Five](/wiki/Philadelphia_Big_5 "Philadelphia Big 5") member has its own boathouse.[[238]](#cite_note-245) Philadelphia hosts numerous local and collegiate rowing clubs and competitions, including the annual [Dad Vail Regatta](/wiki/Dad_Vail_Regatta "Dad Vail Regatta"), the largest [intercollegiate rowing](/wiki/College_rowing_(United_States) "College rowing (United States)") event in North America with more than 100 participating U.S. and Canadian colleges and universities;[[239]](#cite_note-246) the annual [Stotesbury Cup Regatta](/wiki/Stotesbury_Cup "Stotesbury Cup"), which is billed as the world's oldest and largest rowing event for high school students;[[240]](#cite_note-247)[[241]](#cite_note-248) and the [Head of the Schuylkill Regatta](/wiki/Head_of_the_Schuylkill_Regatta "Head of the Schuylkill Regatta").[[242]](#cite_note-249) The regattas are held on the [Schuylkill River](/wiki/Schuylkill_River "Schuylkill River") and organized by [Schuylkill Navy](/wiki/Schuylkill_Navy "Schuylkill Navy"), an association of area rowing clubs that has produced numerous [Olympic rowers](/wiki/Rowing_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Rowing at the Summer Olympics").[[243]](#cite_note-250)

The [Philadelphia Spinners](/wiki/Philadelphia_Spinners "Philadelphia Spinners") were a professional [ultimate](/wiki/Ultimate_(sport) "Ultimate (sport)") team in [Major League Ultimate](/wiki/Major_League_Ultimate "Major League Ultimate") (MLU) until 2016. The Spinners were one of the original eight teams of the [American Ultimate Disc League](/wiki/American_Ultimate_Disc_League "American Ultimate Disc League") (AUDL), which was founded in 2012. They played at [Franklin Field](/wiki/Franklin_Field "Franklin Field") and won the inaugural AUDL championship and the final MLU championship in 2016.[[244]](#cite_note-251) The MLU was suspended indefinitely by its investors in December 2016.[[245]](#cite_note-252) As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philadelphia&action=edit), the [Philadelphia Phoenix](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phoenix_(AUDL) "Philadelphia Phoenix (AUDL)") continue to play in the AUDL.[[246]](#cite_note-253)

Philadelphia is home to the [Philadelphia Big 5](/wiki/Philadelphia_Big_5 "Philadelphia Big 5"), a group of five [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball") programs, including [La Salle](/wiki/La_Salle_University "La Salle University"), [Penn](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania"), [Saint Joseph's](/wiki/Saint_Joseph%27s_University "Saint Joseph's University"), [Temple](/wiki/Temple_University "Temple University"), and [Villanova](/wiki/Villanova_University "Villanova University") universities.[[247]](#cite_note-254) The sixth NCAA Division I school in Philadelphia is [Drexel University](/wiki/Drexel_University "Drexel University"). La Salle won the 1954 championship of the [NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament").[[248]](#cite_note-255) Villanova won the [1985](/wiki/1985_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "1985 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"),[[249]](#cite_note-256) [2016](/wiki/2016_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "2016 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament"),[[250]](#cite_note-257) and [2018](/wiki/2018_NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "2018 NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament")[[251]](#cite_note-258) NCAA Division I men's basketball tournaments. Philadelphia will be one of the eleven US host cities for the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup").[[252]](#cite_note-259)

## Law and government

See also: [Philadelphia City Hall](/wiki/Philadelphia_City_Hall "Philadelphia City Hall")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Independence_Hall_4.jpg/250px-Independence_Hall_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Independence_Hall_4.jpg)

[Old City Hall](/wiki/Old_City_Hall_(Philadelphia) "Old City Hall (Philadelphia)") at 5th and [Chestnut Street](/wiki/Chestnut_Street_(Philadelphia) "Chestnut Street (Philadelphia)"), Philadelphia's town hall from 1800 to 1854

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art_2025-03_-_Philly_flag_01.jpg/250px-Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art_2025-03_-_Philly_flag_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art_2025-03_-_Philly_flag_01.jpg)

Flag of Philadelphia in front of the [Philadelphia Museum of Art](/wiki/Philadelphia_Museum_of_Art "Philadelphia Museum of Art")

Philadelphia County is a [legal nullity](/wiki/Legal_nullity "Legal nullity"). All county functions were assumed by the city in 1952.[[253]](#cite_note-Agency_History-2000-260) The city has been coterminous with the county since 1854.[[62]](#cite_note-Consolidation_Act_of_1854-63)

Philadelphia's 1952 [Home Rule](/wiki/Home_Rule "Home Rule") [Charter](/wiki/Municipal_charter "Municipal charter") was written by the City Charter Commission, which was created by the [Pennsylvania General Assembly](/wiki/Pennsylvania_General_Assembly "Pennsylvania General Assembly") in an act of April 1949, and a city ordinance of June 1949. The existing [city council](/wiki/City_council "City council") received a proposed draft in February 1951, and the electors approved it in an election held in April 1951.[[254]](#cite_note-Charter_Commission-1967-261) The first elections under the new Home Rule Charter were held in November 1951, and the newly elected officials took office in January 1952.[[253]](#cite_note-Agency_History-2000-260)

The city uses the [strong-mayor](/wiki/Mayor%E2%80%93council_government#Strong-mayor_form "Mayor–council government") version of the mayor–council form of government, which is led by one mayor in whom [executive authority](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") is vested. The mayor has the authority to appoint and dismiss members of all boards and commissions without the approval of the city council. Elected [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large"), the mayor is limited to two consecutive four-year terms, but can run for the position again after an intervening term.[[254]](#cite_note-Charter_Commission-1967-261)

### Courts

See also: [District Attorney of Philadelphia](/wiki/District_Attorney_of_Philadelphia "District Attorney of Philadelphia"), [United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Third_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit"), and [United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Eastern_District_of_Pennsylvania "United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania")

[Philadelphia County](/wiki/Philadelphia_County,_Pennsylvania "Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania") is coterminous with the [First Judicial District of Pennsylvania](/wiki/First_Judicial_District_of_Pennsylvania "First Judicial District of Pennsylvania"). The Philadelphia County [Court of Common Pleas](/wiki/Pennsylvania_courts_of_common_pleas "Pennsylvania courts of common pleas") is the [trial court](/wiki/Trial_court "Trial court") of [general jurisdiction](/wiki/General_jurisdiction "General jurisdiction") for the city, hearing [felony](/wiki/Felony "Felony")-level criminal cases and civil suits above the minimum jurisdictional limit of $10,000. The court has [appellate jurisdiction](/wiki/Appellate_jurisdiction "Appellate jurisdiction") over rulings from the [Municipal](/wiki/Philadelphia_Municipal_Court "Philadelphia Municipal Court") and Traffic Courts, and some administrative agencies and boards. The trial division has 70 commissioned judges elected by the voters, along with about one thousand other employees.[[255]](#cite_note-262) The court has a family division with 25 judges[[256]](#cite_note-263) and an orphans' court with three judges.[[257]](#cite_note-264)

As of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philadelphia&action=edit), the city's [District Attorney](/wiki/District_Attorney_of_Philadelphia "District Attorney of Philadelphia") is [Larry Krasner](/wiki/Larry_Krasner "Larry Krasner"), a Democrat.[[258]](#cite_note-265) The last Republican to hold the office is [Ronald D. Castille](/wiki/Ronald_D._Castille "Ronald D. Castille"), who left in 1991 and later served as the Chief Justice of the Pennsylvania Supreme Court from 2008 to 2014.[[259]](#cite_note-266)

The [Philadelphia Municipal Court](/wiki/Philadelphia_Municipal_Court "Philadelphia Municipal Court") handles traffic cases, misdemeanor and felony criminal cases with maximum incarceration of five years, and civil cases involving $12,000 or less ($15,000 in real estate and school tax cases), and all landlord-tenant disputes. The municipal court has 27 judges elected by the voters.[[260]](#cite_note-267)

Pennsylvania's three [appellate courts](/wiki/Appellate_court "Appellate court") also have sittings in Philadelphia. The [Supreme Court of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Pennsylvania "Supreme Court of Pennsylvania"), the court of last resort in the state, regularly hears arguments in [Philadelphia City Hall](/wiki/Philadelphia_City_Hall "Philadelphia City Hall").[[261]](#cite_note-268) The [Superior Court of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Superior_Court_of_Pennsylvania "Superior Court of Pennsylvania") and the [Commonwealth Court of Pennsylvania](/wiki/Commonwealth_Court_of_Pennsylvania "Commonwealth Court of Pennsylvania") also sit in Philadelphia several times a year.[[262]](#cite_note-269)[[263]](#cite_note-270) Judges for these courts are elected at large.[[264]](#cite_note-271) The state Supreme Court and Superior Court have deputy [prothonotary](/wiki/Prothonotary "Prothonotary") offices in Philadelphia.[[265]](#cite_note-272)[[266]](#cite_note-273)

Philadelphia is home to the federal [United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Eastern_District_of_Pennsylvania "United States District Court for the Eastern District of Pennsylvania") and the [Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Third_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit"), both of which are housed in the [James A. Byrne United States Courthouse](/wiki/James_A._Byrne_United_States_Courthouse "James A. Byrne United States Courthouse").[[267]](#cite_note-274)[[268]](#cite_note-275)

### Politics

See also: [List of mayors of Philadelphia](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Philadelphia "List of mayors of Philadelphia"); [Philadelphia City Council](/wiki/Philadelphia_City_Council "Philadelphia City Council"); and [Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania § Politics](/wiki/Philadelphia_County,_Pennsylvania#Politics "Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Musical_Fund_Hall_Philly.jpg/250px-Musical_Fund_Hall_Philly.jpg)](/wiki/File:Musical_Fund_Hall_Philly.jpg)

[Musical Fund Hall](/wiki/Musical_Fund_Hall "Musical Fund Hall") at 808 [Locust Street](/wiki/Locust_Street "Locust Street") hosted the first nominating [Republican National Convention](/wiki/Republican_National_Convention "Republican National Convention") in [1856](/wiki/1856_Republican_National_Convention "1856 Republican National Convention")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Councilmember_Cherelle_Parker.jpg/250px-Councilmember_Cherelle_Parker.jpg)](/wiki/File:Councilmember_Cherelle_Parker.jpg)

[Cherelle Parker](/wiki/Cherelle_Parker "Cherelle Parker"), ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)")), the 100th and current [Mayor of Philadelphia](/wiki/Mayor_of_Philadelphia "Mayor of Philadelphia")

The current mayor is [Cherelle Parker](/wiki/Cherelle_Parker "Cherelle Parker") who won the election in November 2023.[[269]](#cite_note-276) Parker's predecessor, [Jim Kenney](/wiki/Jim_Kenney "Jim Kenney"), served two terms from 2016 to January 2024.[[270]](#cite_note-277) Parker is a member of the [Democratic Party](/wiki/United_States_Democratic_Party "United States Democratic Party"). For over seven decades, since 1952, every [Philadelphia mayor](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Philadelphia#List_of_Mayors "List of mayors of Philadelphia") has been a Democrat.

[Philadelphia City Council](/wiki/Philadelphia_City_Council "Philadelphia City Council") is the legislative branch which consists of ten council members representing individual districts and seven members elected [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large"), all of whom are elected to four-year terms.[[271]](#cite_note-278) Democrats are currently the majority and hold 14 seats including nine of the ten districts and five at-large seats. Republicans hold one seat: the [Northeast-based](/wiki/Northeast_Philadelphia "Northeast Philadelphia") Tenth District. The [Working Families Party](/wiki/Working_Families_Party "Working Families Party") holds two at-large seats making them the council's minority party. The current council president is [Kenyatta Johnson](/wiki/Kenyatta_Johnson "Kenyatta Johnson").[[272]](#cite_note-279)

Philadelphia's political structure consists of a system of wards and divisions. There are 66 wards with 11 to 51 divisions each for a total of 1703 divisions. Each division elects two committee people who are supposed to live within the division boundaries, and committee people select a leader for their ward.[[273]](#cite_note-280) Democrats and Republicans elect their own committee people every four years. The committee person's role is to serve as a point of contact between voters and party officials and help get out the vote.[[274]](#cite_note-281) Most wards are closed which means the ward leader makes sole endorsement decisions; open wards allow committee people to weigh in on these decisions.[[275]](#cite_note-282) There are groups and individuals who are working to elect ward leaders who promote an open ward system.[[276]](#cite_note-283)[[277]](#cite_note-284)
